Subject: Pre-Final Geotechnical Design Report for The New Kapalama Container
Terminal Wharf and Dredging Project, Honolulu Harbor, Oahu, Hawaii
Dear Mr. Kokubun:
Please find attached the Pre-Final Geotechnical Design Report for the subject project. This report
was prepared to provide geotechnical design input and results of our evaluations of seismic
hazards related to the improvement of The New Kapalama Container Terminal Wharf structures.
The geotechnical design input and recommendations are based on the results of field
investigation and laboratory testing program completed and provided to us by Geolabs, Inc.
(Geolabs).
Please submit this report to the State of Hawaii Department of Transportation (H-DOT) and any
other agencies for their review. Review comments from H-DOT and others will be incorporated
into a final report.
We appreciate the opportunity to work with you on this project. If you have any questions, please
do not hesitate to call us.
Sincerely,
EARTH MECHANICS, INC.

1.0 INTRODUCTION
1.1 Purpose
This report has been prepared by Earth Mechanics, Inc. (EMI) to provide geotechnical design
recommendations and evaluation of seismic hazards related to the container wharf structure and
development of new bulkhead for the New Kapalama Container Terminal Wharf and Dredging
Project at Honolulu Harbor, Oahu, Hawaii. The site location map is shown in Figure 1-1. The
geotechnical recommendations provided in this report are only applicable to the container whatf
structure and the new bulkhead piers, which include ground improvement. In addition,
geotechnical recommendations for the westside bulkhead wall are also provided in this report.
Geotechnical design recommendations regarding backland structures and earthwork are provided
in a separate geotechnical report prepared by Geolabs, Inc. (Geolabs, 2014). Site-specific seismic
evaluation for the project was documented in a separate EMI report (EMI, 2014).
This work was performed under a subcontract with Moffatt & Nichol (M&N).
1.2 Project Description
As a part of the New Kapalama Container Terminal Wharf and Dredging Project, the former
Kapalama Military Reservation property at Honolulu Harbor is proposed to be redeveloped into
a new shipping container terminal to handle current and projected cargo volumes. The project
site is located in the Honolulu Harbor, on the south end of the island of Oahu. The approximately
90-acre Kapalama site is bounded on the west by Sand Island Access Road, on the north by
Auiki Street, on the east by Young Brothers’ inter-island barge terminal, and on the south by the
harbor waters Kapalama Basin (Figure 1-1).
As part of the redevelopment, dredging and wharf and bulkhead development activities are
proposed to be performed at Piers 42 and 43, Pier 41, and Pier 40F. Based on the information
provided by M&N designers, the proposed improvements at the new Pier 42 and 43 consist of
the following: (1) Dredging from federal project limit line to face of new Pier to a draft elevation
of -43 ft MLLW, and (2) Construction of a deep draft wharf along the Kapalama channel with
berthing capacity to accommodate two container ships. This will involve filling in Snug Harbor
and Railo Slip areas and dredging in the area fronting the container yard to achieve sufficient
water depth at the new wharf. The overall site plan is provided in Figure 1-2. Existing harbor
bottom in Snug Harbor and Rail Slip will be raised to about El. +10 ft MLLW behind the
waterside sheet pile/king pile bulkhead. We understand that the wharf will be able to handle
vessels of down to El. -39 ft MLLW draft and the length of wharf developed will be long enough
to accommodate a 730-foot vessel and a 900-foot vessel simultaneously.
The development of new bulkhead at Pier 41 consists of dredging to a draft elevation of -40 ft
MLLW along the face of the pier and filling behind the new sheet pile bulkhead to bring the
proposed grade to about El. +10 ft MLLW. The clear distance between the new Pier 41 and the
existing Pier 40, located on the east side, is about 300 feet. The intended use of Piers 40 and 41 is
for loading and unloading of barges. We understand that up to 40 ton capacity top pick
operations are proposed for loading and unloading of containers on barges.
Earth Mechanics, Inc.
Geolechnical & Carthguake Eaginecring
In addition, the proposed improvements at Pier 40F also consist of dredging to a draft elevation
of -40 ft MLLW along the face of the pier and filling between the new sheet pile bulkhead and
the existing slope to bring the proposed grade to about El. +10 ft MLLW.
Based on the recent information provided by M&N designers, we understand that the existing
slope located at the westernmost part of the project will need to be dredged in order to maintain
the required dredged depth (down to El. -40 ft MLLW) along the entire new Pier 42 and 43
wharf to handle vessels. Due to presence of the existing Sand Island Bascule Bridge abutment, a
new 100-foot long bulkhead wall will be required to retain the earth at the proximity of the
bridge abutments. This wall is located in north-south direction. The wall height varies along the
wall; the maximum unsupported wall height is about 49 feet at north end of the wall, near the
container wharf.
1.3 Proposed Improvements
Based on the recent information provided by M&N designers, the proposed improvements in the
new shipping container terminal consist of: (1) construction of a container terminal wharf
structure at Piers 42 and 43, (2) development of a new bulkhead structure at Pier 41, (3)
development of a new bulkhead structure at Pier 40F, and (4) development of a new kingpile
wall at westside of the project. The overall site plan is provided in Figure 1-2. Locations of the
proposed structures are shown on Figure 1-3.
At Piers 42 and 43, a pile-supported wharf structure, containing 100-ton maximum capacity
bollards, with sheet pile bulkhead system and tie-backs are proposed to be constructed. The total
length of the new Pier 42 and 43 wharf is about 1,846 feet. The waterside and landside carne
rails are located at 100 feet apart on-center spacing. For the new Piers 42 and 43 container wharf
located within the Snug harbor portion, both landside and waterside crane rails are supported by
king pile/sheet pile walls. The south portion of the Sung Harbor (i.e., within the container wharf
footprint) will be filled using processed concrete debris and processed coralline/fill materials,
while the north portion of the Sung Harbor will be filled using dredged materails within the
project. Fill materials that are placed within the container wharf footprint of the Snug harbor will
be improved. Wick drains will be needed on the north portion of the Sung Harbor fill (i.c., north
of the landside crane rail). The surcharge will be placed for the entire fill area.
For the new Piers 42 and 43 container wharf located outside of Snug harbor portion, the landside
and waterside crane rails are supported by cast-in-drilled-hole (CIDH) piles; the ground
improvement method is considered behind the sheet pile bulkhead wall. The Rail Slip area at the
east end of the container wharf will be filled using processed coralline/fill materials. Fill
materials that are placed within the container wharf footprint of the Rail Slip will be improved.
Wick drains will not be needed on for the Rail Slip fill. The surcharge will be placed for the
entire Rail Slip fiil area.
At Pier 41, a new bulkhead pier with a sheet pile/king pile bulkhead system and tie-backs is
proposed to be developed. The total iength of the new Pier 41 bulkhead is about 1,023 feet. The
ground will be improved behind the bulkhead wall. Existing grade will be raised to about El. +10
ft MLLW behind the bulkhead. The tie-rod deadman will be supported by CIDH piles.
Earth Mechanics, Inc.
Grolechmeal & Earthquake Engineering
Similar to Pier 41, about a 300-foot long new bulkhead pier is proposed to be developed at Pier
40F We understand that the existing slope in front of the new bulkhead will be dredged down
about El. -40 ft MLLW. Existing slope behind the bulkhead will be filled using processed
coralline materails to raise to about El. +10 ft MLLW. The backland ground will be improved.
Wick drains will not be needed on for the Pier 40F fill. The surcharge will be placed for the Pier
40F fill area. The tie-rod deadman will be supported by CIDH piles.
An about 100-foot long new bulkhead wall is proposed to be constructed at the westernmost part
of the project, near the existing Sand Island Bascule Bridge abutment. This bulkhead wall
comprised of king piles. In order to maintain the required dredged depth to handle vessels along
the entire Piers 42 and 43, the existing slope in front of the bulkhead is proposed to be dredged
down about El. -40 ft MLLW. The sloping backland ground exists behind this bulkhead wall.
Due to space constraints, the backland ground within project boundary behind the bulkhead wall,
located north of Wall LOL Sta. 0+40 [1.c., un-supported wall height (H) is greater than 20 feet] is
proposed to be improved.
14 Scope of Work
EMP’s scope of work is as follows:
e Review available geotechnical information for the site and vicinity.
¢ Review field and laboratory test results and soil strength parameters provided by
Geolabs.
e Evaluate site-specific seismic hazards including liquefaction potential] and seismically-
induced settlement during a seismic event.
e Provide ground improvement recommendations to mitigate liquefaction potential and to
provide adequate lateral capacity for foundation piles.
e Evaluate static and seismic earth pressures for the design of bulkhead walls.
e Review axial pile capacity analyses performed by Geolabs.
e Evaluate lateral capacity of piles supporting crane rails and tie-rod deadman.
e Evaluate lateral earth pressure on the bulkhead wall due to crane loading.
¢ Evaluate long-term soil settlement and provide wick drain, surcharge, and settlement
period recommendations.
e Provide site development recommendations.
¢ Develop construction recommendations.
e Prepare this report documenting our findings, conclusions, and recommendations.

2.0 FIELD EXPLORATION AND LABORATORY TESTING
2.1 Available Subsurface Information
We understand that the available subsurface information consists of boring logs and geology
information in the project vicinity were reviewed by Geolabs as part of the preparation of the
project-specific geotechnical investigation.
2.2 +Project-Specific Geotechnical] Investigation
A project-specific geotechnical investigation consisted drilling, sampling, and logging of twenty
land borings (designated as Boring Nos. 6 through 25) and five off-shore borings (designated as
Boring Nos. 1 through 5), was performed by Geolabs in 2013. The purpose of the exploration
was to log subsurface conditions and collect soil samples from locations near the project site.
One seismic cone penetration testing (SCPT) was also performed in Boring No. 7 for seismic
shear wave velocity profiling. The boring location map and the boring logs provided by Geolabs
are included in Appendix A.
The land and off-shore borings were drilled using truck-mounted and skid-mounted drilling
equipments, respectively, equipped with rotary coring tools. Soil samples were collected from
borings by means of split-spoon drive samplers; the Standard Penetration Test (SPT) and the
Modified Califomia Drive (MCD) samplers were used to collect smali disturbed and relatively
undisturbed samples, respectively. The SPT sampler (2.0-inch OD) and MCD sampler (3-inch
OD) were driven using a 140 pound automatic hammer falling 30 inches down a total depth of
18 inches or until refusal. Core samples of the coral/rock formations encountered at the site were
obtained using diamond core drilling techniques in general accordance with ASTM D 2113.
The off-shore borings were drilled to depths of about 27.5 to 74.6 feet below the mudline, while
the land borings were extended to depths of about 50 to 122.5 feet below the existing ground
surface. Details of the project-specific borings such as the boring/CPT number, date performed,
northing, easting, ground elevation, groundwater elevation, and total depth of the borings are
were provided in a separate Geotechnical Engineering Exploration Report prepared by Geolabs
(Geolabs, 2014).
2.3. Laboratory Testing
Laboratory tests on selected soi] samples recovered from project-specific soil borings were
performed by Geolabs. The following laboratory tests were performed:
e In-Situ Moisture and Density (ASTM D-2937 and D-2216)
e Sieve Analysis (ASTM C-117 and C-136)
e Atterberg Limits (ATM D-4318)
¢ Direct Shear (ASTM D-3080)
e Unconsolidated Undrained Triaxial (ASTM D-2850)
e Unconfined Compression (ASTM D-7012)
Earth Mechanics, Inc.
Gevtechrical & Earthquake Engineering
¢ Modified Proctor Compaction (ASTM D 1557)
e California Bearing Ratio (ASTM D-1883)
¢ Soil Corrosivity (SW-9045C, SM-2510B, and EPA-300.0)
The information obtained from first five tests was used for soil type identification and fine
content correction for liquefaction analyses. The direct shear and triaxial tests were performed to
obtain the shear strength of the in-situ soil. The unconfined compression test was performed on
selected rock cores to evaluate the unconfined compressive strength of the rock formation
encountered. The soil corrosivity tests (pH, minimum resistivity, sulfate content, and chloride
content) were used to assess the hazard of corrosion attack on structural foundations in contact
with site soils.
The modified proctor compaction test was performed on bulk samples of the near-surface soils to
evaluate the relationship between the moisture content and the dry density of the near-surface
soils as fill materials. The CBR test was performed on bulk samples of the near-surface soils to
evaluate the strength characteristics for pavement subgrade support. These test results were not
used in preparation of the recommendations included in this report.
The types and distribution of the laboratory tests performed are shown on the boring log. The in-
situ dry densities, moisture contents, percent passing No. 200 sieve, Atterberg limits, and pocket
penetrometer results are shown on the boring logs. Results of laboratory testing and other details
are provided in a separate Geotechnical Engineering Exploration Report prepared by Geolabs
(Geolabs, 2014),
Earth Mechanics, Inc.
Geolachnical & Earthquaka Engineenng
Lee
3.0 GEOLOGICAL AND SEISMIC CONSIDERATIONS
3.1 Regional Geology
The Island of Oahu was built by the extrusion of basaltic lavas from the Waianae and Koolau
shield volcanoes. The older Waianae Volcano is estimated to be middle to late Pliocene in age
and forms the bulk of the western third of the island. The younger Koolau Volcano is estimated
to be late Pliocene to early Pleistocene (Ice Age) in age and forms the majority of the eastern
two-thirds of the island. Waianae became extinct while Koolau Volcano was still active, and its
eastern flank was partially buried below Koolau lavas banking against its eastern flank. After a
long period of volcanic inactivity, during which time erosion incised deep valleys into the
Koolau Shield, volcanic activity returned with a series of lava flows followed by cinder and tuff
cone formations. These series are referred to as the Honolulu Volcanic Series.
The project site is on the coastal plain of Southern Oahu. The coastal plain was built on the
eroded flanks of the Koolau Volcano, which forms the eastern two-thirds of the Island of Oahu.
The coastal plain was built by extensive accumulation of alluvium derived from erosion of the
volcano, interbedded with coral reefs and associated deposits.
During the Pleistocene Epoch (Ice Age), sea levels fluctuated in response to the cycles of
continental glaciation. Most of the coastal plains were developed during the Pleistocene Epoch
when the sea levels fluctuated significantly. As the glaciers grew and advanced, less water was
available to fill the oceanic basins such that sea levels fell below the present stands of the sea.
When the glaciers melted and receded, an excess of water became available such that the sea
levels rose to above the present sea.
The processes of erosion and deposition were affected by these glacio-eustatic sea level
fluctuations. When the sea level was low, the erosional base level was correspondingly lower,
and valleys were carved to depths below the present sea level. When the sea level was high, the
erosional base level was raised such that sediments accumulated at higher elevations.
The project site generally is underlain by deposits of calcareous sediments and alluvium. The
deposition of soils at the site is likely associated with the nearby Kapalama Stream/Basin,
lagoonal deposits from old fish ponds and Honolulu Harbor, and coralline formation. A surface
layer of man-made fill was placed over these deposits to extend the shoreline for the
development of Honolulu Harbor within the last century.
3.2 Site Seismicity
The project site is in a region with high seismic activity. The magnitude and rate of occurrence of
earthquakes in some parts of Hawaii Islands are comparable to the areas close to the largest
faults in Califomia. However, the mechanism of the earthquakes in Hawaii is different. The
largest earthquakes occur due to mobile active flanks of the volcanos on the island of Hawaii.
The south side of the island has the highest seismic/voleanic activity.
Historical earthquake epicenter maps show widespread seismicity throughout the Hawaii Islands.
The largest historical earthquakes in the islands were two earthquakes in 1868 with intensity-area
magnitude MI of 7.0 and 7.9, and a 1975 earthquake with teleseismic surface wave magnitude
Earth Mechanics, Inc.
Geolectaical & Earthquake Engineesieg
10
MS of 7.2. Figure 2 shows the locations of the largest earthquakes in the region. Since 1868
approximately fifty earthquakes with moment magnitude Mw of 6 or larger have been recorded.
Most of Mw 6 and larger earthquakes occurred on the south side of the island of Hawaii.
Seismicity decreases considerably along the island chain toward northwest of the island of
Hawaii.
As part of the National Seismic Hazard Map program, the United States Geological Survey
(USGS) has published seismic hazard estimates for various regions in the United States since
1996 (Frankel et al., 1996). The hazard maps are based on probabilistic seismic hazard analysis
(PSHA) methodology. Seismic hazard maps for the 48 conterminous states have been published
in 1996, and updated in 2002 and 2008 (Petersen et al., 2008). The latest update in 2008
incorporates NGA models in PSHA. For Hawaii, the maps were initially published in 1998
(Klein et al., 1998), and have not been updated since. The 1998 maps provide estimated peak
ground acceleration (PGA) and spectral accelerations at 0.2, 0.3, and 1.0 second, for 475- and
2,475-yr return periods. These maps were developed for firm-rock site condition, equivalent to
an average shear wave velocity in top 30 m (Vs30) of 760 m/s.
Klein et al. (2001) paper on seismic hazard in Hawaii includes detailed discussion on seismic
source model, earthquake catalog and attenuation relationships used by USGS for developing
1998 maps. This study was performed using an earthquake catalog spanning from 1868 to 1997,
and mainly relied on attenuation equations from 1997 USGS national seismic hazard study.
3.3 Stratigraphy
As provided in the Geotechnical Engineering Exploration Report prepared by Geolabs (Geolabs,
2014), the following stratigraphic units are anticipated within the Kapalama Terminal
Development project site, consisting of soil and rock materials of varying character and
engineering properties. The following stratigraphic units are listed in the general order of
geologic age, beginning with the oldest unit and ending with the youngest unit:
Koolau Basalt (Tkb)
Older Alluvium (Qa)
Coralline Deposits (Qls)
Recent Alluvium (Ra)
Fill (Rf)
The following sections briefly describe the general characteristics of each of the above
stratigraphic units based on our literature research, past experience with similar materials, and
the field exploration and laboratory testing program conducted on selected samples.
3.3.1 Koolau Basalt (Tkb)
Although Koolau Basalt is not encountered within the boring drilled depths, it is the oldest
geologic unit, and consists of pahochoe flows of basaltic lavas and may have a mantle of cobbles
and boulders, or residual soils, overlying the top of the basalt rock formation. In addition, it is
anticipated that various types of volcanic rock sub-components, such as clinker layers and lava
tubes, also may be encountered within the basalt rock formation.
Earth Mechanics, Inc.
Gearechnical & Earthquake Engineering
11
Structurally, Koolau Basalt and its subunits are among the strongest of all the units encountered
or anticipated within the project limits. Therefore, the basalt formation and its related residual
soils and saprolites have high strength characteristics and moderate to low permeability in an
undisturbed state.
3.3.2 Older Alluvium (Qa)
Older alluvial deposits are generally very stiff to hard silty and clayey soils that originally were
deposited in alluvial environments, buried over long periods of time by more recent deposits to
become somewhat consolidated, and then subsequently uncovered at the ground surface by
erosion or other geologic processes. This unit generally overlies the Koolau Basalt and consists
of terrigenous sediments, which have been transported by stream action from the upper reaches
of the streams.
Gravel lenses representing buried stream channel are sometimes encountered. Portions of the
Older Alluvium unit contain some weathered basaltic cobbles and boulders. These coarse-
grained parts of the unit also may contain artesian groundwater from the basal fresh water system
or as a sub-aquifer. The deposits may resemble saprolite soil deposits but are clearly
differentiable by the layered characteristics.
Older Aljuvium materials generally exhibit low permeability (with the exception of the coarse-
grained basaltic cobbles and boulders) and high strength with generally low settlement
characteristics. Frequently, these materials show a moderate to high shrink/swell potential when
subjected to fluctuations in moisture content.
3.3.3 Coralline Deposits (Qis)
This unit consists of fossil remnants of coral-algal reefs and their related detrital debris deposits.
In Hawaii, the detrital deposits tend to be the dominant portion of any coralline rock assemblage.
The detrital deposits are weakly to strongly cemented coralline debris consisting of gravel, sand,
and silt sized clastic fragments. Some of the coralline fragments were dissolved and the solution
cavities filled with cemented and/or uncemented fine sands and silts.
Due to the granular nature of the majority of the coral-algal deposits and the variable degree of
cementation frequently present, this formation generally has high to moderate in-situ
permeability and strength characteristics.
Coralline detritus is one of the coralline deposit subunits consisting of granular detritus broken
from reef formations. It is generally medium dense to dense sandy gravel and gravelly sands.
Silts and clays are only present in minor or trace amounts. Detritus also may be weakly cemented
with occasional moderate to strong cementation.
Coral formation is another subunit that is composed of coral and calcareous algae in their
original growth positions or detrital deposits, which have become moderately to well cemented.
Earth Mechanics, Inc.
Genlechnical & Earinquake Enginegsiag
12
3.3.4 Recent Alluvium (Ra)
These soils are generally the result of sedimentation in a very low energy environment such as an
estuary or bay. Frequently, the soils assigned to this unit accumulated very rapidly under water
and might not have been exposed to air. Therefore, there is a tendency to encounter under-
consolidated sediments, which settle under the weight of minimal overburden or structural
loading. Recent alluvium is generally found within some stream channels and bordering some
stream banks, at the mouths (outlets) of streams, and within broad valley floors and basin
features surrounded by hills. These soils also may be found in proximity to swamp and tidal flat
environments.
The soils assigned to this unit generally are considered to have low in-situ permeability and have
low strength characteristics and the tendency for excessive settlement to occur in structures
overlying soils of this unit. The following subunits are generally encountered in the recent
alluvium.
Recent Alluvium: This subunit generally consists of tan to brown silty clays with varying
amounts of sands and infrequent pockets of gravel and cobbles. The consistency is generally in
the range of very soft to medium stiff.
Lagoonal Deposit: This subunit generally consists of light gray to medium gray sandy silts or
silty sands in a very soft to soft consistency and/or very loose to loose relative density. Gravel
sized fragments are rare. Frequently, the silts are organic in origin and may have a mild to strong
organic odor.
Estuarine/Marsh Deposit: This subunit generally consists of dark gray and brown silts and
clays with fibrous organic materials in a very soft to soft consistency. This soil unit contains
traces of fine to medium grained sands. They are characterized by a moderate to strong organic
odor and may contain small fragile mollusk shells.
3.3.5 Fill (Rf
This stratigraphic unit represents low lying areas which were filled during urbanization. The
majority of these low lying areas is underlain at relatively shallow depths by recent alluvium and
was originally marsh areas. In areas underlain by the recent alluvium, the fill materials generally
consist of clayey silts and sandy silts with stiff to hard consistencies. The fills only form a thin
crust over the soft alluvial soils.
The quality and composition of the fill materials are variable. These fills were frequently placed
without proper compaction control. Therefore, it is difficult to make any general statement
regarding their engineering properties or construction in these areas.
Groundwater in the fill areas generally is expected to occur as a basal water table near sea level.
However, higher perched groundwater tables may be encountered.
Earth Mechanics, Inc.
Geotechnical & Earinquake Engineer ng
13
3.4 General Subsurface Conditions
As provided in the Geotechnical Engineering Exploration Report prepared by Geolabs (Geolabs,
2014), the information gathered from the project-specific land borings (B-6 through B-25)
indicated that the project site is generally underlain by surface fills and lagoonal materials over
coralline deposits. The surface fills consisted of granular coralline gravel and sand with
occasional silty or clayey soils, extending to depths of up to 9 feet below existing ground surface.
The lagoonal materials were comprised of soft/loose silty or sandy soils with thickness up to
about 21 feet. It should be noted that the lagoonal material was not encountered in the borings
drilled generally near the northeastern boundary of the property. The coralline deposits primarily
consisted of dense coral formation, sandstone/limestone and medium dense coralline detritus
with localized loose/soft pockets and highly variable consistency extending to the maximum
depth drilled of approximately 122.5 feet below the existing ground surface. The results of our
laboratory testing indicate that the consistency of the coralline formation may range from soft to
hard with highly variable unconfined compressive strength between about 40 and 2,600 pounds
per square inch (psi).
In the project-specific off-shore borings (B-1 through B-5), the mudline was encouneterd
generally at about 30 to 45 feet below the water surface. The borings B-1 through B-3 that were
drilled along Piers 42 and 43 encountered soft harbor sediments over coralline deposits
extending to depths of approximately 69 to 104 feet below water. The subsurface soils
encountered in the off-shore borings B-4 and B-5 that were drilled along Pier 41 consisted of soft
harbor sediments and/or very stiff alluvium overlying coralline deposits extending to depths of
approximately 57.5 to 114 feet below water. It should be noted that the mudline encountered at
the boring B-5 was about 45 feet below water, which may be localized and caused by adjacent
vessel’s mooring and/or berthing activities.
It should be noted that the above soil description is general and is intended to describe the
subsurface in very broad terms. The soil descriptions above should not be construed to indicate
that the subsurface profile is uniform and that soil is homogeneous within the project area. For
details on the stratigraphy at each boring location, refer to the log of borings included in
Appendix A. Detailed descriptions of the materials encountered from the project-specific field
exploration are also provided in the Geotechnical Engineering Exploration Report prepared by
Geolabs (Geolabs, 2014).

Geolabs. Cross sections showing generalized subsurface profiles along Piers 42 and 43, and Pier
41 prepared by Geolabs are included in Appendix A. The idealized soil profile and design
strength parameters used for geotechnical evaluations are provided in Table 3-1.

3.6 Groundwater
Approximate
Elevations
(ft MLLW)
Predominant Soil Type
As provided in the Geotechnical Engineering Exploration Report prepared by Geolabs (Geolabs,
2014), during the the project-specific land soil borings in 2013, groundwater was encouneterd at
depths of about 4.2 to 8.5 feet below the existing ground surface, corresponding to elevations
from +3 to -1 ft MLLW, at the time of our field exploration. However, due to the proximity of
the project site to the Pacific Ocean, it should be noted that groundwater levels are expected to
change with tidal fluctuations. Seasonal precipitation, storm surge condition, surface water
runoff, and other factors may also influence the groundwater levels at the project site.
Based on the above information and the observed high water groundwater elevation, the design
groundwater elevation of +2 ft MLLW provided by Geolabs was conservatively used for
liquefaction analysis.
3.7 _—_ Levels of Seismic Hazards Used in Design
Seismic hazard recommendations for the Kapalama Container Wharf are based on the site-
specific seismicity study performed by EMI for the subject project. Three level seismic criteria
that are based on the ASCE Seismic Standards for Piers and Wharves (ASCE 61-14) (ASCE,
2014), which is currently under preparation, are considered: Operating Level Earthquake (OLE),
Contingency Level Earthquake (CLE), and Design Earthquake (DE).
The DE defined in ASCE 61-14 (ASCE, 2014) is based on ASCE 7-05 (ASCE, 2006), which is
adopted in IBC, 2009. In addition to using IBC, 2009, the bulkhead was also designed for IBC,
2012, as directed by H-DOT. IBC, 2012 is based on ASCE 7-10 (ASCE, 2011). Per Section
11.8.3.1 of ASCE 7-10 (ASCE, 2011), if the site is improved to eliminate liquefaction under
MCE level earthquake, DE ground motion can be used to determine dynamic seismic lateral
earth pressures on bulkhead walls. However, if liquefaction is not mitigated, the effects of
liquefaction under MCE level earthquake must be accommodated in the design.
, ae Earth Mechanics, Inc.
Geolechnical & Barthquake Engineering
15
3.8 Site Classification and Peak Ground Acceleration Recommendations
Subsurface materials predominantly consist of shallower liquefiable granular soils between
approximate El. +2 ft and -11 ft MLLW at the project site under design earthquakes. Due to the
presence of liquefiable materials, the site is classified as Class ‘F’ for seismic evaluations of the
proposed structures per ASCE 7-05 guidelines (ASCE, 2006). In order to mitigate liquefaction
potential and to improve lateral resistance of piles, ground improvement is proposed to be
performed behind the sheet pile/king pile bulkhead walls and piles supporting crane loads for
container wharf / bulkhead pier structures and Site Class ‘D’ would be appropriate for the
improved ground condition.
It should be noted that the design of the bulkhead requires only the peak ground accelerations
(PGAs) corresponding to OLE, CLE, and DE. Three levels of ground motions that are used for
the design of wharf structures and corresponding PGAs and equivalent earthquake magnitudes
for improved ground condition (Site Class D) for each level of earthquake are provided in Table
3-2. Details of methodology and findings of this seismic hazard study are provided in a separate
seismic study report prepared by EMI (EMI, 2014).

2014). As defined, DE is two-third of MCE and the corresponding PGA for DE level is 0.239.
However, since the PGA for DE level based on ASCE 61-14 (ASCE, 2014) is 0.255g, we
recommend using this value, instead of 0.239g as a conservative measure.

and earthquake magnitude of 7.5 was used for liquefaction potential and seismic settlement
evaluations for the MCE level.
Earth Mechanics, Inc.
Geotechmeal & Earthquake Engineering
16
3.9.1 Liquefaction Potential
Liquefaction is defined as the transformation of a granular material from a solid to a liquefied
state as a consequence of increased pore-water pressure and reduced effective stress. Loose to
medium dense granular materials have a tendency to become compacted and densified when
subjected to cyclic loadings. This tendency induces increased pore-pressure which reduces the
effective stress of the soil and significantly reduces the soil’s strength. The reduction in shear
strength of soil may lead to foundation failure, large shear deformations or even flow failure to
occur depending on the level of shaking and sloping condition of the ground.
The liquefaction potential of the saturated, granular materials below the water table is evaluated
using the procedures outlined by Seed et al. (1983) and updated by NCEER (1997), Martin et al.
(1999), and Youd et al. (2001). The field SPT N-values are normalized with all the
recommended corrections. The available cyclic shear resistance of the potentially liquefiable
soils estimated based on the normalized SPT N-value is compared to the cyclic shear stress that
would be induced by an expected level of earthquake.
Plasticity index (PI) is a better indicator of liquefaction susceptibility or cyclic softening of
claylike fine-grained soils. Per Boulanger and Idriss (2006), soils with PI>7 are considered not
susceptible to liquefaction; they also provide a methodology to estimate the effects of cyclic
softening of fine-grained soils. According to Bray and Sancio (2006), soils with PI>18 are not
susceptible to liquefaction and soils with 18>PI>12 are systematically more resistant to
liquefaction.
Based on the liquefaction analyses performed using project-specific soil borings and peak ground
accelerations, loose/medium dense submerged fill/Lagoonal deposits materials encountered
between approximate elevations of +2 ft and -11 ft MLLW are expected to be susceptible to
liquefaction under OLE, CLE, DE, and MCE earthquake events. As indicated in the
Geotechnical Engineering Exploration Report prepared by Geolabs (Geolabs, 2014), materials
(coral formation/sandstone/limestone/coralline detritus) encountered below approximate El. -11
ft MLLW are not expected to be susceptible to liquefaction due to the local geology setting at the
project site. As discussed in Section 3.6, the design groundwater of El. +2 ft MLLW was used
for liquefaction analyses based on the information provided by Geolabs and the recorded
groundwater depth in the project-specific soil borings. The liquefaction calculations are provided
in Appendix C.
3.9.2 Seismically-Induced Settlement
The liquefied soils reconsolidate during and after earthquake shaking, leading to ground
settlement. The scale of ground surface settlement depends on several factors such as
characteristics of the soil, level and duration of shaking, and extent of the liquefaction zone. For
soils with PI<7, the seismically-induced settlements were estimated using the procedures
outlined by Tokimatsu and Seed (1987) for the project-specific soil borings and presented in
Table 3-3. The seismically-induced settlement calculations are provided in Appendix C.
Earth Mechanics, Inc.
Geotechnical & Eartfiquake Engineering
17
The estimated seismically-induced settlement values are approximate and may vary depending
on several factors, such as layered nature of subsurface soils, amount of fine contents, and excess
pore pressure dissipation pattern of fine-grained soils.
Seismically-induced settlements of up to 7 inches are estimated under CLE, DE, and MCE
earthquake events. Except for Boring B-16, seismically-induced settlements of up to 5.2 inches
are estimated under OLE earthquake event. It should be noted that the seismically-induced
settlement estimates are based on the existing soil conditions. If the ground improvement is
implemented to mitigate liquefaction potential, the scismically-induced settlements are
anticipated to be reduced significantly.

4.0 ANALYSES, CONCLUSIONS, AND RECOMMENDATIONS
The objectives of this report are to evaluate subsurface conditions and geotechnical hazards
based on the results of geotechnical field investigation and laboratory testing performed by
Geolabs and to provide geotechnical recommendations to mitigate seismic hazards and to design
Piers 42 and 43 container wharf, Pier 41 and Pier 40F sheetpile/kingpile bulkhead pier structures,
and westside bulkhead wall, and to develop Snug Harbor, Rail Slip, and Pier 40F areas. In
addition, our evaluation on lateral pile capacities, lateral earth pressures, and long-term soil
settlements are also included.
4.1 Subsurface Conditions and Liquefaction Potential under Existing Conditions
The project site generally consists of loose to medium dense fill / Lagoonal deposits materials
potentially liquefiable below water table down to approximate El. -11 ft MLLW under OLE,
CLE, DE, and MCE earthquake events. As indicated in the Final Geotechnical Engineering
Exploration Report prepared by Geolabs (Geolabs, 2014), coralline detritus/coral
formation/sandstone/limestone materials encountered below approximate El. -11 ft MLLW are
not expected to be susceptible to liquefaction under OLE, CLE, DE, and MCE earthquake
events.
4.2 Lateral Earth Pressure Methodology
Seismic Earth Pressures: The most frequently used method for the calculation of the seismic soil
forces acting on a retaining structures is a static approach developed in the 1920’s by Mononobe-
Okabe. The Mononobe-Okabe analysis is an extension of the Coulomb sliding-wedge theory
taking into account horizontal inertial forces acting on the backfill. The Mononobe-Okabe
equations can be used for cohesionless materials only. Since the fill / Lagoonal deposits
materials at the project site are liquefiable during seismic events and is modeled as a cohesive
material, the standard Mononobe-Okabe equation cannot be used. Therefore, the slope stability
computer program SLIDE 5.0 (Rocscience, 2006) was used to estimate the seismic earth
pressures. Soil behind the sheet pile wall was modeled down to the point of rotation wall and soil
below the mudline in front of the sheet pile (on the passive side) down to the point of rotation
was eliminated in the SLIDE model, since the primary purpose of analyses is to determine the
pressure applied on the sheet pile wall during seismic event by the soil above the point of
rotation of the sheet pile wall. Horizontal inertial forces representing the seismic event were
generated within the soil mass behind the sheet pile wall by applying seismic coefficients of
0.065g, 0.182g, 0.21g, and 0.235g under OLE, CLE, DE, and MCE earthquake events,
respectively. Earth pressure estimates were based on procedures and guidelines outlined in the
NCHRP Report 611 (Transportation Research Board, 2008) and Permanent International
Association for Navigation Congresses (PIANC, 2001). Seismic coefficients to evaluate the earth
pressures and inertial forces were determined according to PIANC (2001) guidelines. Static earth
pressure and incremental seismic pressure were applied against the sheet pile wall (towards
landside). The incremental seismic pressure was adjusted until the factor of safety became one.
This incremental pressure corresponds to the incremental seismic earth pressure on the sheet pile
wall when the soil mass behind the wall become marginally stable.
Earth Mechanics, Inc.
Geotecnnical & Eaithquake Engineering
19
Hydrodynamic Pressures: During seismic shaking, the free water in front of the structure exerts a
cyclic dynamic loading on the wall; the critical mode occurs during the phase when suction
pressure is applies on the wall (Westergaard, 1933; PIANC, 2001). The solution developed by
Westergaard (1933) is for the case of a reservoir retained by a semi-infinite, smooth faced
concrete dam and subjected to a horizontal earthquake motion. The fundamental period of the
concrete dam is assumed to be much smaller than the fundamental period of the earthquake, so
that the acceleration for the massive structure is approximated as the acceleration of the
earthquake motion along the rigid base. This allows the problem of a very stiff concrete dam to
be simplified to the case of a rigid vertical face moving at the same horizontal acceleration as the
horizontal base acceleration. Since the waterfront structures at Piers 42 and 43 container wharf,
Piers 41 and 40F bulkheads and westside bulkhead will be a sheet pile/king pile wall system,
which does not have a smooth flat face on the waterside compared to the rigid straight smooth
concrete dam structure, hydrodynamic forces calculated using Westergaard equation is expected
to be lower for the sheet pile walls. Therefore, one half the value of hydrodynamic forces under
OLE, CLE, DE, and MCE earthquake events were recommended.
Due to liquefaction potential of subsurface materials during seismic events, the seismic earth
pressures are significantly higher and require hefty sheet pile (king piles) wall system with
significant tie-backs. The tie-back anchors also needs to be designed to withstand high lateral
forces and requires deadman to be supported by long piles that are embedded well in to the dense
material below the liquefiable materials. It is estimated by the design team that improving the
backland ground to mitigate liquefaction and increase lateral capacity of deadman pile would be
a cost-effective option.
4.3 Proposed Structures and Improvements
Based on the information provided by M&N designers, we understand that the following
foundation alternatives and ground improvements as described below are considered as part of
this project: (1) a new Piers 42 and 43 container wharf (within and outside of Snug Harbor); (2)
development of a new bulkhead pier at Pier 41; (3) development of a new bulkhead pier at Pier
40F; and (4) a new bulkhead wall at the westernmost part of the project site, located near the
Sand Island Bascule Bridge abutment.
4.3.1 Container Wharf at Piers 42 and 43 - Snug Harbor Portion
The proposed improvements at Piers 42 and 43 Snug Harbor area comprised of an about 191-
foot long pile-supported container wharf with sheet pile/king pile bulkhead system (i.e., Skyline
section HZ 1180 MC-26 / AZ. 26-700 or equivalent) and_3.25-inch diameter steel tie-rods spaced
at 9 feet on-center. The tie-rods are anchored to a 8-foot wide landside crane rail beam supported
by steel sheet pile/king pile bulkhead system, located approximately 112 feet behind the face of
wharf (Figure 4-1). The waterside crane rail beam is supported by 3-foot diameter cast-in-drilled-
hole (CIDH) piles spaced at 9 feet on-center. The existing mudline elevation along the face of
the waterside bulkhead is about -40 ft MLLW. In order to handle vessels, the existing mudline is
proposed to be dredged down about El. -45 ft MLLW. Existing harbor bottom behind the
waterside bulkhead is proposed to be raised to about El. +10 ft MLLW. Details of site
development at Snug Harbor are provided in Section 4.8. The backland ground between 6 feet
north of waterside sheet pile/king pile bulkhead wall and 10 feet north of landside sheet pile/king
Earth Mechanics, Inc.
Geotechnical & Earthquake Engmneerning
20
pile bulkhead is proposed to be improved using Stone Column (SC) method. A minimum area
replacement ratio (ARR) (i.e., ratio of the area of stone columns as a percentage of total
improved area) of 20% is recommended. The top elevation of SC columns is recommended to be
+5 ft MLLW and the bottom of the SC is expected to vary (Figure 4-1).
4.3.2 Container Wharf at Piers 42 and 43 — Outside of Snug Harbor Portion
The proposed improvements at Piers 42 and 43, outside of Snug Harbor area, comprised of a
pile-supported container wharf with sheet pile/king pile bulkhead system (i.e., Skyline section
HZ 1180 MC-26 / AZ 26-700 or equivalent) and 3.25-inch diameter steel tie-rods spaced at 9
feet on-center. The total length of the container wharf (including the Snug Harbor portion) is
about 1,846 feet long. The tie-rods are anchored to a 17-foot wide landside crane rail beam,
supported by two rows of 4-foot diameter CIDH piles staggered at 18-foot on-center spacing.
The centerline of this landside crane rail beam is located approximately 112 feet behind the face
of wharf (Figure 4-2). The waterside crane rail beam is supported by 3-foot diameter CIDH piles
spaced at 9 feet on-center. In order to handle vessels, the existing slope in front of the waterside
bulkhead is proposed to be dredged down about El. -45 ft MLLW. Existing grade is proposed to
be raised to about El]. +10 ft MLLW behind the waterside sheet pile bulkhead. The backland
ground between 6 feet north of sheet pile/king pile bulkhead wall and 10 feet north of landside
CIDH pile is proposed to be improved using SC method. A minimum ARR of 20% is
recommended. The top and bottom elevations of SC columns are recommended to be +5 ft
MLLW and -15 ft MLLW, respectively (Figure 4-2).
4.3.3 Bulkhead Pier for Loading and Unloading of Barges at Pier 41
The proposed improvements at about 1,024-foot long Pier 41 comprised of sheet pile/king pile
bulkhead system (i.e., Skyline section HZ 1180 MD-14 / AZ 19-700 or equivalent) and 3-inch
diameter steel tie-rods spaced at 9 feet on-center. The tie-rods are anchored to a 17-foot wide
landside deadman/pile cap, supported by two rows of 4-foot diameter CIDH piles staggered at
18-foot on-center spacing. The centerline of this deadman/pile cap is located approximately 78.5
feet behind the face of wharf (Figure 4-3). In order to handle vessels, the existing slope in front
of the bulkhead is proposed to be dredged down about El. -40 ft MLLW. Existing grade is
proposed to be raised to about El. +10 ft MLLW behind the bulkhead. The backland ground
between 10 feet north of sheet pile/king pile bulkhead wall and 10 feet north of landside CIDH
pile is proposed to be improved using SC method. A minimum ARR of 20% is recommended.
The top and bottom elevations of SC columns are recommended to be +5 ft MLLW and -15 ft
MLLW, respectively (Figure 4-3).
4.3.4 Bulkhead Pier at Pier 40F
Similar to Pier 41, the proposed improvements at about 300-foot long Pier 40F comprised of
sheet pile/king pile bulkhead system (i.e., Skyline section HZ 1180 MD-14 / AZ 19-700 or
equivalent) and 3-inch diameter steel tie-rods spaced at 9 feet on-center. The tie-rods are
anchored to a 17-foot wide landside deadman/pile cap, supported by two rows of 4-foot diameter
CIDH piles staggered at 18-foot on-center spacing. The centerline of this deadman/pile cap is
located approximately 73.5 feet behind the face of wharf (Figure 4-4). We understand that the
existing slope in front of the bulkhead is proposed to be dredged down about El. -40 ft MLLW.
Earth Mechanics, Inc.
Geotechnical & Earthnuake Enginecring
21
Existing grade is proposed to be raised to about El. +10 ft MLLW behind the bulkhead. The
backland ground between 10 feet north of sheet pile/king pile bulkhead wall and 10 feet north of
landside CIDH pile is proposed to be improved using SC method. Based on our experience on
past projects, a minimum ARR of 20% is recommended. The top elevation of SC columns is
recommended to be +5 ft MLLW and the bottom of the SC is expected to vary (Figure 4-4).
4.3.5 Bulkhead Wall Near Sand Island Bascule Bridge Abutment
A new bulkhead wall is proposed to be constructed at the westernmost part of the Pier 42 and
43, near the existing Sand Island Bascule Bridge abutment. The total length of the bulkhead wall
is about 100 feet. This bulkhead wall comprised of king piles (i.¢., Skyline section HZ 1180 MC-
23 or equivalent). In order to maintain the required dredged depth to handle vessels along the
entire Piers 42 and 43, the existing slope in front of the bulkhead is proposed to be dredged down
about El. -40 ft MLLW. The sloping backland ground exists behind the bulkhead. The northem
portion of the wall, located north of Wall LOL Sta. 0+40 [i.e., un-supported wall height (H) is
greater than 20 feet] is supported by steel tie-rod anchored to the Piers 42 and 43 waterside
bulkhead system and additional king piles installed along the extension of Piers 42 and 43
waterside bulkhead. No tie-rods are used for the southern portion of the wall, located south of
Wall LOL Sta. 0+40 (i.e., H is are Jess than 20 feet). Due to space constraints, the backland
ground behind the bulkhead wall, located north of Wall LOL Sta. 0+40, within project boundary
is proposed to be improved using SC method. The proposed improvements at the westside
bulkhead is shown in Figure 4-5. Based on our experience on past projects, a minimum ARR of
20% is recommended for SC improvements. The SC columns will be installed from exiting
grades and the bottom elevation of SC columns is recommended to be -20 ft MLLW.
4.4 Recommendations for Piers 42 and 43 Container Wharf —- Within Snug Harbor
4.4.1 Global Stability
The SC improved soil is expected to buttress the sheet pile bulkhead and global stability (deep-
seated failure below the proposed sheetpile/kingpile system) is not considered to be a design
issue during both static and seismic conditions.
4.4.2 Lateral Earth Pressures
Static earth pressures for temporary condition (during construction) with unimproved ground
condition are provided in Figure 4-6 and Figure 4-7. Static lateral earth pressure for long term
condition with improved ground condition is provided in Figure 4-8. Seismic lateral earth
pressures under OLE, CLE, and DE earthquake events for long term condition with improved
ground condition are provided in Figure 4-9, Figure 4-10, and Figure 4-11, respectively.
Appropriate lateral pressure on the bulkhead wall from live/concentrated loads should be
considered for static and seismic cases, as indicated on the lateral earth pressure diagrams. The
horizontal movement at which the maximum passive pressure is expected to be fully mobilized is
1 percent of the total embedment below final mudline. No arching factor is needed for the HZ
1180 MC-26 / AZ 26-700 or equivalent.
Earth Mechanics, Inc.
Geotecnnizal & Earthquake Engineering
22
4.4.3 Axial Pile Capacity and Axial Pile Stiffness
Ultimate compressive axial capacities of a king pile for the waterside sheet pile/king pile
bulkhead (HZ 1180 MC-26/AZ 26-700 or equivalent) and supporting landside crane rail, and a
3-foot diameter CIDH pile supporting water side crane rail are provided by Geolabs. Details of
the axial pile capacity calculations are provided in a separate Geotechnical Engineering
Exploration Report prepared by Geolabs (Geolabs, 2014).
Using on the pile tip movement of 0.3 inches (at El. -60 ft MLLW) at the ultimate axial
compressive load capacity provided by Geolabs and the estimated axial deformation of pile, an
axial pile stiffness value of 3,200 kips/inch is recommended at the pile top for a single 36-inch
CIDH pile. For a single king pile, an axial pile stiffness value of 1,000 kips/inch at the pile top is
recommended using on the pile tip movement (at El. -80 ft) at the ultimate axial compressive
load capacity provided by Geolabs, and the estimated axial deformation of pile. Due to
uncertainties associated with the development of axial springs, it recommended to use upper-
bound and lower-bound spring values of axial stiffness by multiplying the axial stiffness value
by 2.0 and 0.5, respectively.
4.4.4 Lateral Pile Capacity for the King Pile
Lateral capacity of a landside crane rail king pile (HZ 1180 MC-26/AZ 26-700 or equivalent)
was determined using the computer program LPILE (Ensoft, 2010). The p-y curves for sandy
soils were estimated using the API criteria (API, 2000). The preliminary lateral pile analysis for
the king pile was performed for improved ground condition (ie., using stone columns
techniques) under static condition for a “free” pile head condition. Based on the information
provided by M&N, pile head and pile tip elevations were assumed to be at +4 ft and -80 ft
MLLW, respectively. Based on the procedures outlined in the Ensoft Group 7.0 software
Technical Manual (Ensoft, 2006), the average group efficiency factor of 0.75 was estimated for a
on-center spacing between two adjacent king piles equal to about 94 inches. The soil parameters
used were provided by Geolabs. The lateral pile results are summarized in Table 4-1. The results
of lateral pile analysis are provided in Appendix C.

4.5 Recommendations for Piers 42 and 43 Container Wharf - Outside of Snug Harbor
4.5.1 Global Stability
Based on the proposed configuration with the SC improved soil and idealized soil profile from
the site-specific soil borings, the global stability (deep-seated failure below the proposed sheet
piles/king piles) is not considered to be a design issue during both static and seismic conditions.
4.5.2 Lateral Earth Pressures
Static lateral earth pressure for long term condition with improved ground condition is provided
in Figure 4-12. Seismic lateral earth pressures under OLE, CLE, and DE earthquake events for

4.5.3 Axial Pile Capacity and Axial Pile Stiffness at Pile Top
Ultimate compressive axial capacities of a king pile for the waterside sheet pile/king pile
bulkhead (HZ 1180 MC-26/AZ 26-700 or equivalent) and 4-foot and 3-foot diameter CIDH
piles, supporting landside and water side crane rails, respectively, are provided by Geolabs.
Details of the axial pile capacity calculations are provided in a separate Geotechnical
Engineering Exploration Report prepared by Geolabs (Geolabs, 2014).
Using on the pile tip movement of 0.3 inches (at El. -60 ft MLLW) provided by Geolabs and the
estimated axial deformation of pile, an axial pile stiffness value of 3,200 kips/inch is
recommended at the pile top for a single 3-foot CIDH pile. Using on the pile tip movement of 0.4
inches (at El. -60 ft MLLW) provided by Geolabs and the estimated axial deformation of pile, an
axial pile stiffness value of 3,800 kips/inch is recommended at the pile top for a single 4-foot
CIDH pile. For a single king pile, an axial pile stiffness value of 1,000 kips/inch is recommended
at the pile top using on the pile tip movement (at El. -80 ft MLLW) at the ultimate axial
compressive load capacity provided by Geolabs, and the estimated axial deformation of pile. Due
to uncertainties associated with the development of axial springs, it recommended to use upper-
bound and lower-bound spring values of axial stiffness by multiplying the axial stiffness value
by 2.0 and 0.5, respectively.

4.5.5 Passive Resistance from Deadman/Pile Cap
Passive resistance from the dead man/pile cap can be used to resist lateral forces. Assuming that
the distance between face of pile cap and face of sheet pile is about 101 feet, passive earth
pressure resultant forces on the face of pile cap are estimated to be about 15 kips and 9 kips
under static and seismic conditions, respectively. The passive earth pressure resultant forces
should be applied at ‘h/3’ feet above the bottom of the pile cap (‘h’ is the pile cap thickness in
Earth Mechanics, Inc.
Geniechnica:
4 & Earthquake Engineering
25
feet). Full passives pressures can be assumed to be mobilized at a displacement equivalent to 2
percent of the pile cap thickness.
4.6 Recommendations for Pier 41 Bulkhead
4.6.1 Global Stability
Based on the proposed configuration with the SC improved soil and idealized soil profile from
the site-specific soil borings, the global stability (deep-seated failure below the proposed sheet
piles/king piles) is not considered to be a design issue during both static and seismic conditions.
4.6.2 Lateral Earth Pressures
Static lateral earth pressure for long term condition with improved ground condition is provided
in Figure 4-16, Seismic lateral earth pressures under OLE, CLE, and DE earthquake events for
long term condition with improved ground condition are provided in Figure 4-17, Figure 4-18,
and Figure 4-19, respectively. Appropriate lateral pressure on the bulkhead wall from
live/concentrated loads should be considered for static and seismic cases, as indicated on the
lateral earth pressure diagrams. The horizontal movement at which the maximum passive
pressure is expected to be fully mobilized is 1 percent of the total embedment below final
mudline. To account for “arching effects" below the sheet pile (AZ Section) tip elevation, the
active (including surcharge loadings) and passive pressures should be reduced by multiplying
them by a factor of 0.6 below the sheet pile (AZ Section) tip elevation.
4.6.3 Axial Pile Capacity and Axial Pile Stiffness at Pile Top
Ultimate compressive axial capacities of a king pile for the waterside sheet pile/king pile
bulkhead (HZ 1180 MD-14/AZ 19-700 or equivalent) and a 4-foot diameter CIDH pile
supporting the landside tie rod deadman are provided by Geolabs. Details of the axial pile
capacity calculations are provided in a separate Geotechnical Engineering Exploration Report
prepared by Geolabs (Geolabs, 2014).
Using on the pile tip movement of 0.4 inches (at El. -60 ft MLLW) provided by Geolabs and the
estimated axial deformation of pile, an axial pile stiffness value of 3,800 kips/inch is
recommended at the pile top for a single 4-foot diameter CIDH pile. For a single king pile, an
axial pile stiffness value of 1,000 kips/inch is recommended at the pile top using on the pile tip
movement (at El. -80 ft MLLW) at the ultimate axial compressive load capacity provided by
Geolabs, and the estimated axial deformation of pile. Due to uncertainties associated with the
development of axial springs, it recommended to use upper-bound and lower-bound spring
values of axial stiffness by multiplying the axial stiffness value by 2.0 and 0.5, respectively

4.11 Recommendations for Concrete Deck
Based on the information provided by the designers, we understand that a 21-inch thick concrete
deck over 12-inch thick crushed miscellaneous base (CMB) layer will be used within the Piers
42-43 container wharf site. Relevant geotechnical recommendations for the concrete deck are
provided in the following sections.
4.11.1 Vertical Modulus of subgrade reaction (kj):
Provided the 12-inch thick CMB layer is placed below the bottom of the concrete deck and the
following earthwork is performed, the vertical modulus of subgrade reaction (k;) of 350 pci is
recommended for the concrete deck design. The vertical modulus of subgrade reaction value (k;)
is recommended for 1-foot x 1-foot concrete deck. For a B feet wide x B feet long concrete deck,
the modulus of subgrade reaction (kp) can be estimated using the following formula:
34
kp = k; x [((B+1)/2B],
where k, - Modulus of subgrade reaction for a 1-foot x 1-foot concrete deck; ky - Modulus of
subgrade reaction for a B feet x B feet concrete deck.
4.11.2 Earthwork Considerations
The proposed CMB should be compacted to at least 95 percent relative compaction.
The existing ground should be excavated down to El. +5 ft MLLW (ie., top of stone column
improvement). The excavation should be backfilled using granular, non-expansive soils. Up to
the bottom of the proposed CMB layer, the backfill should be placed in loose lifts not exceeding
8 inches in thickness and compacted to at least 95 percent of the maximum dry density
determined using ASTM D 1557. The bottom of the excavation should be firm and unyielding.
In area where the excavated bottom is found to be soft, soil should be excavated an additional 12
inches and replaced with soil with less than 50 percent fines and compacted to at least 90 percent
of maximum dry density.
4.12 Recommendations for Culverts
Geotechnical recommendations for the proposed 3-foot wide x 5-foot deep reinforced concrete
box (RCB) culvert, located within stone column areas, are provided below. It should be noted
that al] other remaining recommendations for the RCB culvert located outside the stone column
area, provided by Geolabs (Geolabs, 2014), are applicable to the RCB culvert located within
stone column areas.
4.12.1 Lateral Earth Pressures
RCB culverts can be designed using Caltrans criteria [i.c., Section 6 of the Caltrans Bridge
Design Specifications (2000)]. For Static Active Lateral Earth Pressures, Caltrans criteria
requires that RCB culverts be designed using two dead load conditions:
(1) Using an Equivalent Fluid Weight (EFW) of 140 pcf to determine vertical pressure at the top of the
box combined with an EFW of 100 pef to determine lateral earth pressure against the walls; and
(2) Using an EFW of 140 pef to determine vertical pressure combined with a 35 pef EFW to determine
lateral earth pressure.
In addition to lateral earth pressures, lateral pressure due to surcharge loads, if any, Should be
included in the design.
4.12.2 Bearing Capacity
Based on a culvert width of 3 feet and an embedment depth of 13 feet (i.e., culvert invert El, = -3
fi MLLW and finished grade El. = +10 ft MLLW), for the proposed RCB within stone column
areas, net maximum allowable bearing capacity of 2,500 psf is recommended. Total and
differential settlements of the RCB culvert are estimated to be less than 1 inch and 0.5 inch,
respectively.

4.12.3 Earthwork Considerations
The existing ground should be excavated to a depth of at least 1 foot below the bottom of the
proposed RCB culvert. The excavation should be performed undermeath the entire footprint of
the RCB culvert. The horizontal limits of the excavation should extend 1 foot from outer edges
of the RCB culvert. We recommend using fine gravels for backfilling underneath RCB culvert
and compacted to at least 90 percent of maximum dry density. The 1-foot thick fine gravel layer
should be wrapped in a non-woven filter fabric (Mirafi 180N or equal). In order to minimize any
damage, the Contractor may use light weight compaction equipment.
4.12.4 Modulus of Subgrade Reaction (k)
For a 3-foot wide x 5-foot deep RCB culvert within stone column areas, a modulus of subgrade
reaction of about 20 pci can be used.

4.14 Backland Ground Improvement
Subsurface materials are generally surface fills and lagoonal materials over non-liquefiable coral
formation/coralline detritus/limestone/sandstone. The surface fills consisted of loose to medium
granular coralline gravel and sand and lagoonal materials comprised of loose to medium dense
silty or sandy soils are potentially liquefiable down to El. -11 ft MLLW. Three different potential
ground improvement methods as described below are studied to improve the backland subsurface
materials and their advantages and disadvantages are summarized in Table 4-11.
_ = Earth Mechanics, Inc.
eohogs: & Lauroguare Unqunys

Stone-Column (SC): The stone column is constructed by vibrating down a probe to the bottom of
liquefiable materials, filling the newly created hole with crushed rock, and vibrating the crushed
tock to increase its density and diameter as the probe is pulled out the design depth. Stone
columns can be installed using either the wet top feed method or the dry bottom feed method.
The wet method is usually faster, but the dry bottom feed method offers more control over the
placement of the crushed rock and the column diameter. If the percent of fine-grained materials
exceed more than 20 to 25 percent, the hydraulic conductivity of the materials with higher fine
content become too low to permit rapid escape of water during the treatment process. Therefore,
additional measures such as larger stone columns or vertical drains may be needed to increase
the effectiveness of the stone columns. Since the stone-column improvement will be
implemented as design-construct method by the selected contractor, additional measures to
improve the effectiveness of stone columns should be evaluated and adopted by the contractor.
Deep Soil Mixing (DSM): The deep mixing method is an in situ soil mixing technology that
mixes existing soil with cementitious materials using mixing shafts consisting of auger cutting
heads, discontinuous auger flights and mixing paddles. The resulting soil-cement (or other
binders) columns have a higher strength, less compressibility, and lower permeability than native
soils. DSM is the mechanical blending of the in sitv soil with grout sturry or dry binder agents
using hollow-stem paddle mixer. A revolving hollow shaft with mixing paddles is advanced into
the soil. As the mixing tool is advanced, the cement grout is pumped through the hollow stem of
the revolving shaft and discharged laterally along the lower mixing paddle where it is mixed with
native soil. The primary goal is to mix the soil and the cement grout, The kneading action of the
mixing tool blends the cement grout and the soil. When the design depth is reached, the tool is
withdrawn maintaining the rotational speed of the mixing tool. The resulting soil-cement column
will gain high strength with curing. Rebar can be installed if high bending or tensional capacities
are required. Depending on the soil type and method of DSM, spoils generated may range from
10 to 40 percent of the improved volume.
Earth Mechanics, Inc.
Georeene cs? & hacmavaeks Crgirgerng
37
Jet Grouting: The jet grouting (JG) system produces a homogenous soil-cement column by
eroding the soil through injection of high pressure bentonite-cement grout, which solidifies
afterward. JG can be used effectively in most soil types (e.g. soft clays and silts to dense sands
and gravels), and with lesser success in rock-like materials. In a sequence of the typical jet
grouting operations, first, a borehole is drilled to the required depth by rotary or rotary-
percussive methods using water, compressed air, bentonite, or a binder as the flushing medium.
When the founding level is reached, the flushing ports are closed and the binder is injected at a
very high pressure (2,900 per square inch [psi] to 8,700 psi) through the nozzles and into the soil.
Simultaneously, the drill string is rotated and slowly withdrawn. Rotation speeds typically range
between 10 and 30 revolutions per minute, and the withdrawal rates typically vary between 8 and
20 inches/min. Once the withdrawal is completed, a column of improved cemented soil
(soilcrete) will be formed that has higher strength and lower compressibility that unimproved
soils. Depending on the jet grouting system used, the diameter of the column size can vary from
a few feet to as much as ten to twenty feet. Overlapping columns can be constructed using JG
and arranged in a grid pattern to encase problematic soils. Other treatment patterns for JG
include block, wall, grid, column, secant column, secant wall, secant grid, and secant block. The
final engineering properties of the soil mix is controlled by soil type, the design of the mixture
jetted into the soil, and the thoroughness of the mixing of the cement and soil elements, Since the
grout is injected into the void created by erosion, most of eroded materials become spoils
traveling through the annulus of string rods. Typically, 60 to 90 percent of the JG column
volume is generated as spoils.
Among the available ground improvement method, vibro-stone column replacement is
considered more suitable cost-effective method. A minimum area replacement ratio (i.¢., ratio of
the area of stone columns as a percentage of total improved area) of 20% is recommended for the
project. To minimize the impacts due to encountering obstructions, predrilling is recommended
at all stone column locations. The minimum extents of the stone-column improvement behind the
sheet pile walls at Piers 42-43, Pier 41 and Pier 40F are shown in Figure 4-1 through Figure 4-4.
The minimum extent of the stone-column improvement behind the westside bulkhead wall is
shown in Figure 4-5.
4.14.1 Blowcount Requirements of Stone-Column Improved Ground
The purpose of the stone-column ground improvement is to densify the loose to medium dense
subsurface materials that are potentially liquefiable during seismic events down El. -15 ft MLLW
and to increase the lateral capacity of landside piles.
The improvements of subsurface materials are typically measured by SPT blowcounts for this
project. The SPT blowcounts should be corrected for hammer energy, overburden pressure, fine
contents. Based on the liquefaction analyses performed, it is required to achieve over-burden,
hammer energy and fines content corrected SPT blowcounts {(Ni)é0cs} of 29 throughout the
depth in stone-column improved ground. The recommended stone-column improvement zone is
provided in Figure 4-47.
The ground improvement contractor is responsible to design the diameter and spacing of the
stone-columns to achieve the minimum SPT blowcounts required based on the trial test area
results.
Earth Mechanics, Inc.
Searches cal & Lam quake Pog nett ay
38
4.14.2 Test Areas for Trial Stone-Column Layouts
In order to determine the appropriate area replacement ratio, three test areas, approximately 30 ft
wide (measured along the wharf) and covering entire width of proposed stone-column extent, are
recommended (See Figure 4-47). In each test area, three different zone replacement ratios should
be tried by the contractor, starting the first zone with 20 percent area replacement ratio. The first
zone is recommended to be closest to waterside (Figure 4-47), Based on the verification test
results from the first zone, contractor should select appropriate area replacement ratios for the
other two zones in each test area to optimize the stone-column layout locations (area replacement
tatio) to achieve the required densification of subsurface soils. If the trial zones do not meet the
criteria, additional stone-columns should be installed to achieve the required densification in the
trial zones.
4.14.3 Verification Soil Borings and Laboratory Test Requirements-Test Areas
A minimum of one soil boring should be performed per each zone within each test area. Soil
boring should be performed at a location far from all the adjacent stone-columns. Soil boring
should be performed with SPT blowcounts taken at every 5 feet interval from the ground surface
down to a minimum depth of 5 feet below the base of the stone columns.
SPT blow counts should be obtained using automatic safety hammers only. Efficiency of each
hammer should be measured in at least two borings, at every 5 feet interval to a minimum depth
of 5 feet below the base of the stone-columns. A report documenting the results of the energy
measurements should be submitted for review and approval.
In each soil borings, soils that are classified as GM, GC, SM, SC and ML should be tested for
grain size distribution or percent passing #200; soils that are classified as GC, SC, ML, MH, CL-
ML, CL, and CH should be tested for Atterberg Limits.
4.14.4 Verification Soil Borings and Laboratory Test Requirements-Production Area
Soil boring should be performed every 1,600 square feet at a location far from all the adjacent
stone-columns. Soil boring should be performed with SPT blowcounts taken at every 5 feet
interval from the ground surface down to a minimum depth of 5 feet below the base of the stone-
columns.
In each soil borings, soils that are classified as GM, GC, SM, SC and ML should be tested for
grain size distribution or percent passing #200; soils that are classified as GC, SC, ML, MH, CL-
ML, CL, and CH should be tested for Atterberg Limits.
4.14.5 Vibration and Movement Monitoring during Stone~Column Installation
Contractor should monitor vibration during stone-columns installation in vicinity of utilities,
sheet pile bulkhead, and other structures, including structures that have been constructed as part
of this project. Care should be taken to avoid damage to the existing structures due to vibration
from stone column installation operations.
Earth Mechanics, Inc.
Geotechnical & Facthquake Engrigeang
—- —\
poem
39
Contractor should also monitor any settlement, uplift, and lateral movement of the existing
structures and structures that have already been constructed as part of this project, including
sheet piles, roads, utilities, and buildings adjacent to the soil improvement area. If settlement,
uplift, or lateral movement in excess of 0.25 inch is detected, all soil improvement work should
immediately be ceased and the Engineer should be notified. Soil improvement work should not
recommence until corrective measures is implemented by the Contractor to prevent further
settlement, uplift, and lateral movement.
4.15 Idealized Soil Parameters after Ground Improvement
The requirements of stone-column improvement are to densify the loose to medium dense
fillNagoonal materials to dense to very dense state, to mitigate the liquefaction potential within
the improved zone, and increase lateral pile capapcity of landside piles. Idealized soil parameters
are developed based on dense to very dense sandy materials within the improved zone (Table
4-12). It should be noted that soil parameters behind the stone-column improved zone will
remain unimproved with respect to liquefaction during design earthquakes.
