4.16 Soil Corrosivity
Details of laboratory corrosion test results, including pH, minimum resistivity, chloride content,
and sulfate content, and relevant corrosion recommendations for the project site are provided in a
separate Geotechnical Engineering Exploration Report prepared by Geolabs (Geolabs, 2014).
4.17 Construction Considerations
4.17.1 Temporary Excavations
Temporary excavations must be properly shored or sloped to accommodate construction of
foundations. Shoring, if any, should be designed using the soil parameters provided in this report.
As a minimum, shoring should be designed for a uniform lateral earth pressure of not less than
26H psf below 0.2H feet from ground surface, linearly increasing from zero at ground surface
(where H is the height of the excavation in feet). An additional lateral uniform pressure of 72 psf
due to a traffic surcharge should be added to the above lateral earth pressure values, where
applicable. If excavations are to be sloped, maximum depth of excavation should not exceed
more than 20 feet and the slope should not be steeper than 1.5H:1V (Caltrans, 2011). Design and
construction of temporary slopes or shoring should be made by the contractor’s responsibility.
Due to tidal changes, if any excavation extends below El. +3 ft MLLW, it is expected that
groundwater control may be necessary at the location to keep the excavation free from water and
some heaving at the base of the excavation may be expected. If groundwater is encountered
during excavation, the contractor should take appropriate measures to control groundwater.
It should be noted that it is the responsibility of the contractor to oversee the safety of the
workers in the field during construction. The contractor should conform to all applicable
occupational and health standards, rules, regulations and orders established by the State of
California, In addition, other State, County, or Municipal regulations may supersede the
recommendations presented in this section. If a trench shoring design and safety plan is required,
the geotechnical consultant should review the plan to confirm that recommendations presented in
this report have been applied to the design.
4.17.2 Driven Pile Construction
The pile installation procedures should account for pile type, size and length, subsurface
characteristics, hammer characteristics, and allowable pile stresses. Pile drivability analysis
should be performed by the contractor to select appropriate pile-driving equipments for different
pile and sheet piles.
The contractor should perform wave equation analysis to select suitable hammer that delivers
sufficient energy to drive the piles to the required tip elevation. Swinging leads should not be
allowed to guide pile driving hammers. If vibratory hammers are used, pile should be driven with
the vibratory hammer only to within 10 feet of the required tip elevation. An impact hammer
should be used to drive the pile for the remaining length. .
Earth Mechanics, Inc.
Geoltehoical & Carhguake Enginee dng
cc
cote
4]
Since large number of piles to be installed for the subject project, indicator production piles with
PDA measurements are recommended to verify the effectiveness of the pile installation
procedure recommended by the contractor. During indicator production pile installation,
measurements using pile drivability analyzer (PDA) is recommended to confirm the selection of
proper pile-driving equipment, pile drivability, and minimize damage to pile tip during driving.
A total of seven indicator production piles are recommended for this project at the following
locations:
One pile (first pile) at westside bulkhead.
Two piles at Snug Harbor (first pile on the landside and first pile on the waterside)
One pile (first pile) at Piers 42-43 west of Snug Harbor . .
One pile (first pile) at Piers 42-43 east of Snug Harbor
One pile (first pile) at Pier 41
One pile (first pile) at Pier 40F
AwWaRWNH>
Piles driving should terminate at the specified tip elevation except for the following conditions:
(1) If the filler sheet pile reaches refusal in the very dense/hard coral formation/coralline
detritus/limestone/sandstone materials with pile tip within 5 feet above design tip elevation with
2 feet minimum penetration into the very dense/hard coral formation/coralline
detritus/limestone/sandstone materials, it can be cut-off. (2) If the king pile (“HZM” pile)
reaches refusal in the very dense/hard coral formation/coralline detritus/limestone/sandstone
materials with pile tip within 10 feet above design tip elevation, it can be cut-off. The
geotechnical engineer should be consulted if pile refusal is encountered at more than the above-
mentioned depths above the specified tip elevation.
Based on the available soil exploration data, hard driving is expected to be encountered above
the specified pile tip elevation. The Contratcor should be prepared to drive the piles through the
processed coralline materials and very dense/hard soils (coral formation/coralline
detritus/timestone/sandstone). The Contractor should review site-specific soil boring loag to get
familiarized with the existing subsurface conditions so that appropriate means and methods are
used during pile driving.
We recommend predrilling to assist driving the piles through dense/hard soils (coral
formation/coralline detritus/limestone/sandstone). Predrilling should extend deep enough to
assure required minimum king pile embedment. Pre-drilled holes should be no larger than the
depth of king pile.
Jetting is not allowed during the pile driving.
Existing structures located in the vicinity of pile driving should be monitored for movement.
Temporary benchmarks should be established at selected locations along existing sheet piles,
utilities, and other structures adjacent to pile driving locations. The benchmarks should be
monitored twice daily during pile driving operation to detect any settlement, uplift, or lateral
movement of the existing structures/facilities.

4.18 Review of Construction Plans
Recommendations contained in this report are based on preliminary plans. The geotechnical
consultant should review the final construction plans and specifications to confirm that the
general intent of the recommendations contained in this report has been incorporated into the
final construction documents. Recommendations contained in this report may require
modification or additional recommendations may be necessary if the final design deviates
significantly from the present design.
4.19 Geotechnical Observation and Testing
It is recommended that inspection and testing be performed by the geotechnical consultant
during the following stages of construction:
Grading operations, including excavations and placement of compacted fill,
Pile construction,
Stone column installation,
Footing excavation and bottom inspection prior to concrete pouring for footing,
Removal or installation of support of buried utilities or structures, and
When any unusual subsurface conditions are encountered.

2. Uniform active lateral pressure of 0.35q psf between El. -3 ft and El. -40 ft and 0.45q below El. -40 ft should be included for a surcharge loading of q psf, where
q is the appropriate live load during seismic event.
3. The horizontal movement at which the maximum passive pressure is expected to be fully mobilized is 1 percent of the total embedment below final mudiine.
4. No arching factor is needed tor Skyline Section HZ 1180 MC-26 / AZ 26-700 .

5.0 LIMITATIONS
This report is intended for the use of Moffatt & Nichol Engineers (M&N) and its design team
members for The New Kapalama Container Terminal Wharf and Dredging Project at Honolulu
Harbor, Oahu, Hawaii. This report is based on the project as described and the information
provided to us. The geotechnical input and seismic hazard evaluation contained in this report are
based on the boring logs, laboratory test results and site characterization provided to us by
Geolabs, Inc. (Geolabs). Details of the field investigation, laboratory testing, and subsurface
conditions of the site are provided in a separate Geotechnical Engineering Exploration Report
prepared by Geolabs (Geolabs, 2014). This report should be used as a companion to the report
prepared by Geolabs. Soils and subsurface conditions encountered in the exploratory boring are
presumed to be representative of the project site. However, subsurface conditions and
characteristics of soils can vary. Earth Mechanics Inc. (EMI) was not involved in any field
investigation work for the project. The geotechnical input and seismic hazard evaluation
presented in this report are based on the assumption that an appropriate level of quality control
and quality assurance were provided during field investigation. EMI should be notified of any
pertinent changes in the project plans or if subsurface conditions are found to vary from those
described herein. Such changes or variations may require a re-evaluation of the
recommendations contained in this report.
The data, opinions, and recommendations contained in this report are applicable to the specific
design element(s) and location(s) which is (are) the subject of this report. They have no
applicability to any other design elements or to any other locations and any and all subsequent
users accept any and all liability resulting from any use or reuse of the data, opinions, and
recommendations without the prior written consent of EMI.
EMI has no responsibility for construction means, methods, techniques, sequences, or
procedures, or for safety precautions or programs in connection with the construction, for the
acts or omissions of the CONTRACTOR, or any other person performing any of the
construction, or for the failure of any worker to carry out the construction in accordance with the
Final Construction Drawings and Specifications.
Services performed by EMI have been conducted in a manner consistent with that level of care
and skill ordinarily exercised by members of the profession currently practicing in the same
locality under similar conditions. No other representation, expressed or implied, and no warranty
or guarantee is included or intended.
Earth Mechanics, Inc.
Geatechnical & Earthquake Engineering

ROCK DESCRIPTIONS
= 272] FINGER CORAL
. PP
\ BOULDERS | LIMESTONE

COBBLES See
CORAL | VOID/CAVITY
ii ROCK DESCRIPTION SYSTEM
ROCK FRACTURE CHARACTERISTICS
The following terms describe general fracture spacing of a rock:
Massive: Greater than 24 inches apart
xXXKXX
fy Slightly Fractured: 12 to 24 inches apart
Moderately Fractured: 6 to 12 inches apart
Closely Fractured: 3 to 6 inches apart
Severely Fractured: Less than 3 inches apart
an
DEGREE OF WEATHERING
The following terms describe the chemical weathering of a rock:
Unweathered: Rock shows no sign of discoloration or loss of strength.
Slightly Weathered: Slight discoloration inwards from open fractures.
Moderately Weathered: Discoloration throughout and noticeably weakened though not able to break by hand.
Highly Weathered: Most minerals decomposed with some corestones present in residual soil mass. Can be broken by hand.
Extremely Weathered: Saprolite. Mineral residue completely decomposed to soil but fabric and structure preserved.
HARDNESS
The following terms describe the resistance of a rock to indentation or scratching:
Very Hard: Specimen breaks with difficulty after several “pinging” hammer blows.
Example: Dense, fine grain volcanic rock
Hard: Specimen breaks with some difficulty after several hammer blows.
Example: Vesicular, vugular, coarse-grained rock
Medium Hard: Specimen can be broked by one hammer blow. Cannot be scraped by knife. SPT may penetrate by
~25 blows per inch with bounce.
Example: Porous rock such as clinker, cinder, and coral reef
Can be indented by one hammer blow. Can be scraped or peeled by knife. SPT can penetrate by
~100 blows per foot.
Example: Weathered rock, chalk-like coral reef
Crumbles under hammer blow. Can be peeled and carved by knife. Can be indented by finger Plate
pressure.
Example: Saprolite A-0 . 2

APPENDIX B
RESULTS OF AXIAL PILE ANALYSES PROVIDED BY GEOLABS
DRAFT REPORT 12/3/2013 - FOR REVIEW & COMMENT
SECTION 3. DISCUSSION AND RECOMMENDATIONS
replacing with select granular material with a layer of triaxial geogrid, such as
Tensar TX-160 or equivalent, placed at the bottom of the over-excavation.
To reduce risk of potential pavement depression due to consolidation settlement
caused by the new fills over the soft/loose lagoonal soils, we recommend a surcharge
and settlement monitoring program. The surcharge should consist of 4 feet of material
placed for a period of 2 to 3 months in the fill areas where the fill thickness is greater
than 3 feet. Where the fill thickness is less than 3 feet, the surcharge may be eliminated,
but a waiting period should be implemented. The waiting period may consist of placing
select granular material to design finished grade with settlement monitoring for a period
of 2 to 3 months. The settlement monitoring should be conducted optically by a qualified
surveyor and reviewed by Geolabs.
3.1 Drilled Shaft Foundations
Based on our evaluation of the subsurface conditions and the foundation
demands, we recommend supporting the new crane rails on cast-in-place concrete
drilled shafts. The drilled shafts would derive vertical support primarily from friction
between the concrete shaft and the surrounding soils. In general, the end-bearing
component of the drilled shafts has been discounted in our analysis due to difficulties
associated with obtaining a clean bottom during construction in the relatively deep
drilled shafts.
Based on the subsurface conditions encountered at the project site and the
anticipated structural loads, we believe 30-inch to 48-inch diameter drilled shafts may
be installed to support the crane rails. Our recommendations pertaining to the drilled
shaft ultimate axial compressive load capacities with various tip elevations and
diameters are presented in the following table. We assumed the top of drilled shafts at
elevation +10 feet.
W.O. 6831-00 GEOLABS, INC. Page 16
Hawaii « California

A factor of safety of 2.0 may be used in arriving at the allowable compressive
load capacities. The allowable compressive load capacities are for supporting
dead-plus-live loads and may be increased by up to one-third (1/3) when considering
transient loads, such as wind or seismic forces. In general, drilled shafts in groups
should be spaced a minimum of 2.5 times the drilled shaft diameter center-to-center to
avoid further reduction in vertical load capacity due to group action and to facilitate
drilling of the shaft holes.
The load bearing capacities of the drilled shafts will depend largely on the
consistency or relative density of the coralline deposits and quality of the coral formation
within the bearing strata. Because local variations in the subsurface materials tikely will
occur at the site, it is imperative that a Geolabs representative be present during the
drilled shaft operations to confirm the subsurface conditions encountered during the
drilled shaft construction and to observe the installation of the drilled shafts. In addition,
contract documents should include provisions (unit prices) for additional drilling and
extension of the drilled shaft during construction to account for unforeseen subsurface
conditions.
Based on our evaluation of the subsurface conditions and the foundation design
parameters, we anticipate that the drilled shaft installation for the project will require an
experienced drilled shaft subcontractor to install the drilled shaft foundations. Therefore,
consideration should be given to requiring pre-qualification of the drilled shaft
subcontractor for this project. The subsequent subsections address the design and
construction of the drilled shaft foundations, which include the following:
W.O. 6831-00 GEOLABS, INC. Page 17

-
3.1.1 Downdrag Loads |
As mentioned above, the soft and/or loose lagoonal deposits at the project site may
be susceptible to liquefaction during a seismic event at the project site. In the event ]
of seismically induced liquefaction, setttement of the loose lagoonal deposits will
occur at the site. When the loose lagoonal deposits move downward relative to the |
stationary drilled shaft, the downward movement will result in negative skin friction
forces, generally Known as downdrag forces, acting on the drilled shaft. The f
magnitude of the downdrag force depends on the thickness of the liquefiable layer,
relative density of the potentially liquefiable joose lagoonal deposits, and the drilled | |
shaft perimeter.
Due to the potential for liquefaction of the soft and/or loose lagoonal deposits at the
project site, settlement of the soft and/or loose lagoonal deposits will result in
downdrag loading of the deep foundations extending through the potentially
liquefiable materials. Therefore, the allowable compressive load capacities of the
drilled shafts should be reduced by the expected downdrag load in the drilled shaft
foundation analyses.
We understand that a ground improvement program utilizing stone columns may be
implemented to mitigate the potential liquefaction in the loose lagoonal deposits. ‘
Therefore, the downdrag loads may be neglected from the drilled shaft foundation
design presented in this report, assuming the ground improvement method is
effective to mitigate the potential liquefaction at the site. The ground improvements |
will be evaluated by others. —
W.O. 6831-00 GEOLABS, INC. Page 18
Hawaii * California
DRAFT REPORT 12/3/2013 - FOR REVIEW & COMMENT
, SECTION 3. DISCUSSION AND RECOMMENDATIONS
3.1.2 Uplift Load Resistance
In general, uplift loads may be resisted by a combination of the dead weight of the
drilled shaft and by shear along the shaft surface and the adjacent soils.
Considering that the drilled shafts are designed based on adhesion between the
shaft and the surrounding soils, recommendations for the ultimate uplift load
capacities are presented in the following table.
Ultimate Uplift Load Capacity
(kips)
30-Inch = ne 42-Inch 48-inch
too | 1500
The uplift load capacities are for groups of drilled shafts; therefore, a group uplift
resistance factor of 0.55 has been applied to the values provided in the above
table. The uplift load capacities provided include the weight of the drilled shaft.
The uplift load capacity provided above should be used for transient loads only. To
resist sustained uplift loads, a factor of safety of 2.0 should be applied to the uplift
load capacity. The project structural engineer should design and adequately
reinforce the shaft member to carry the tension load, where used.
j 3.1.3 Lateral Load Resistance
Lateral loads imposed on the crane rails may be resisted by a combination of the
passive earth pressure acting against the near-vertical faces of the foundation caps
and/or grade beams and the lateral load capacity of the drilled shafts. Passive earth
pressure against the near-vertical faces of the foundation caps and/or grade beams
may be estimated using an equivalent fluid pressure of 350 and 150 pounds per
Ls square foot per foot of depth (pcf) for above and below groundwater conditions,
respectively. Lateral load resistance contributed by friction between the foundation
caps (when engaged to act as a diaphragm) and the underlying cushion fill

Liquefaction analysis Is performed folowing Seed’s Procedure, outlined by Seed at al. (1985),
Seed and Harder (1990), updated NCEER (1997), Martin et al. (1999) and Youd at al. (2001) The resisting cyclic stress ratio (CRR r) is equal to:
GRR r = CRR;z5 x Cm x K sigma
EmeanE60 = Energy Ratio to correct to standard 60% Energy
Surcharge = Any surcharge on top of the ground (psf} where: Gm = Earthquake Magnitude Correction Factor (Blake, NCEER Table 3, Eq.4)
ace, max = maximum peak acceleration at the ground surface {g's) K sigma = Normatized resistance factor (Fig. 14 from NCEER Liquefaction workshop, Harder Boulanger)
‘Cu = square root of (100 kPa or 1.04427 tsf/effective overburden) CRR;,.s = Resisting Cyclic Stress Ratio. It is a function of the content of fines
The induced cyclic stress ratio {CSR f) by a given peak ground acceleration (acc. max) is: (Figure 2, NCEER Workshop, after Seed et al 1985))
CSR | = 0.66 x ace max. x iotal normal stress x rd / (effective normal stress x g) Factor of Safely, F, is:
FL = CRRr/CSRi

LIQUEFACTION ANALYSIS (QLE},-M = 6.5 ; ACC. = 0.065 g
Liquefaction analysis is performed following Seed’s Procedure, outlinad by Seed et al. (1985),
Sead and Harder (1990), updated NCEER (1997), Martin et al. (1999) and Youd et al. (2001) The resisting cyclic stress ratio (CRR r) is equal to:
CRRr= CRR7s5 x Cm xK sigma
Emean/E60 = Energy Ratio to correct to standard 60% Energy
Surcharge = Any surcharge on top of the ground (psf) where: Cm = Earthquake Magnitude Correction Factor (Blake, NCEER Table 3, Eq.4)
acc. max = maximum peak acceleration at the ground surface (g's) K sigma = Normalized resistance factor (Fig. 14 from NCEER Liquefaction workshop, Harder Boulanger}
‘Cy = square root of (100 kPa or 1.04427 taffeffective overburden) GRR; = Resisting Cyclic Stress Ratio. [tis a function af the content of fines
The induced cyclic stress ratio (CSR §} by a givan peak ground acceleration (acc. max) is: (Figure 2, NCEER Workshop, after Seed et al 1985))
CSR i = 0.65 x acc max. x total normaf stress x rd / (effective normal stress x g) Factor of Safety, F, is:
FL= CRRr/CSRi

Liquefaction analysis is performed following Seed's Procedure, outlined by Seed et al. (1985),
Seed and Harder (1990), updated NCEER (1997), Martin et af. (1999) and Youd et al. (2001) The resisting cyclic stress ratio (CRR 1} fs equal to:
CRRr=CRR;,5x Cm x K sigma
Emean/E60 = Energy Ratio to correct to standard 60% Energy
Surcharge = Any surcharges on top of the ground {psf) where: Cm = Earthquake Magnitude Correction Factor (Blake, NCEER Table 3, Eq.4)
acc, max = maximum peak acceleration at the ground surface (g's) K sigma = Normalized resistance factor (Fig. 14 from NCEER Liquefaction workshop, Harder Boulanger)
‘Cy = square root af (100 kPa or 1.04427 teffeffective overburden) CRR;,5 = Resisting Cyclic Stress Ratio. It is a function of the content of fines
The induced cyclic stress ratio {CSR i} by a given peak ground acceleration (acc. max) is: {Figure 2, NCEER Workshop, after Seed et al 1985)
CSR i= 0.65 x acc max. x total normal stress x rd / (effective normal stress x g) Factor of Safety, F, is:
FL = CRRr/CSRi

Kapafama Container Wharf Project
LIQUEFACTION ANALYSIS (OLE), M = 6.5; ACC. = 0.065 g
Liquefaction analysis is performed following Seed's Procedure, outlined by Seed et al. (4985),
Seed and Harder (1990), updated NCEER (1997}, Martin et al. (1999) and Youd et af. (2001)
EmearvE60 = Energy Ratio to correct to standard BO% Energy
Surcharge = Any surcharge on top of the ground (psi}
acc. max = maximum peak acceleration at the ground surface {9's}
‘Cy = square root of (100 kPa or 1.04427 tsfisffective overburden)
The induced cyclic stress ratio (CSR i) by a given peak ground acceleration (acc. max) is:
CSR i = 0.65 x acc max. x total normal stress x rd / (effective normal stress x g)
EMI Project No. 13-104
Version - 12905
SRR R ARRAN eRee Reneemnnennmmns RRRR Ui ee een nents Meee meee
The resisting cyclic stress ratio (CRR r) is equaf to:
CRRr=CRR;.5x Gm x K sigma
where: Cm = Earthquake Magnitude Correction Factor (Blake, NCEER Table 3, Eq.4}
{ sigma = Normalized resistance factor (Fig. 14 from NCEER Liquefaction workshop, Harder Boulanger)
CRR;,s = Resisting Cyclic Stress Ratio. It is a function of the content of fines
(Figure 2, NCEER Workshop, after Seed et a 1985))
Factor of Safely, F, is:
FL = CRRr/CSRi

Liquefaction analysis is performed following Seed's Procedure, outimed by Seed et al. (1985),
Seed and Harder (4990), updated NCEER (#997), Martin et al. (1999) and Youd et ai. (2001}
EmeanE60 = Energy Ratio ta correct ( standard 60% Energy
Surcharge = Any surcharge on top of the ground (psf)
acc. max = maximum peak acceleration at the ground surface (g's)
‘Cy = square root of (100 kPa or 1.04427 tsfleffective overburden)
The induced cyclic stress ratio (CSR i) by a given peak ground acceleration (acc. max) is:
CSR | = 0.65 x acc max. x total normal stress x rd / (effactive normal stress x g)
EM! Project No, 13-104
The resisting cyclic stress ratio (CRR 1) is equal to:
CRR r= CRR75 x Cm x K sigma
where: Cm = Earthquake Magnitude Correction Factor (Slake, NCEER Table 3, Eq.4}
K sigma = Normalized resistance factor (Fig. 14 from NCEER Liquefaction workshop, Harder Boulanger)
CRR7.s ™ Resisting Cyclic Stress Ratio. It is a function of the content of fires
(Figure 2, NCEER Workshop, after Sead et at 1885))
Factor of Safety, F, is:
FL= CRRriGSRi

LIQUEFACTION ANALYSIS (OLE), M = 6.5 ; ACC. = 0.065 g
Ae en ETE NE TT RE RRR eereeeemenneeett— SannenmemennnuY emerennauerene
Liquefaction analysis is performed folowing Seed’s Procedure, outlined by Seed et al. (1985),
Seed and Harder (1990), updated NCEER (1997), Mastin at al. (1999) and Youd et al. (2004) The resisting cyclic stress ratio (CRR r) is equal to:
GRR r= CRRzs x Cm x K sigma
EmeanE6e = Energy Ratio to correct to standard 60% Energy
Surcharge = Any surcharge on top of the ground (psf} where: Cm = Earthquake Magnitude Correction Factor (Blake, NCEER Table 3, Eq.4)
acc. max = maximum peak acceleration at the grourks surface (g's) K sigma = Normalized resistance factor (Fig. 14 from NCEER Liquefaction workshop, Harder Boulanger)
‘Cn = square root of (100 kPa or 1.04427 tsfleffective overburden) CRRz,.; = Resisting Cyclic Stress Ratio. It is a function of the content of fines
The induced cyclic stress ratio {CSR i} by a given peak ground acceleration (acc. max) is: (Figure 2, NCEER Workshop, after Seed et al 1985)}
CSR | = 0.65 x ace max. x total narmal stress x rd / (effective normal stress x g) Factor of Safely, F, Is:
FL, = CRRrfCSRi

Kapalame Container Wharf Project EMI Project No. 13-104 Version = 12108
LIQUEFACTION ANALYSIS (OLE), M=6.5; ACC. = 0.0659
Liquefaction analysis is performed follawing Seed’s Procedure, outlined by Seed et al. (1985),
Seed and Harder (1990), updated NCEER (1897}, Martin et al. (1999) and Youd et af. (2001} The resisting cyclic stress ratia (CRR r) 5 equat to:
CRR e = CRR;5 x Gro x K sigma
EmearvE60 = Energy Ratio to correct to standard 60% Eneray
Surcharge = Any surcharge on top of the ground (psf) where: Cm = Earthquake Magnitude Correction Factor (Blake, NCEER Table 3, Eq.4}
acc. max = maximum peak acceleration at the ground surface (g's) K sigma = Normalized resistance factor (Fig. 14 from NCEER Liquefaction workshop, Harder Boulanger)
‘Cy = square root of (100 kPa or 1.04427 tsfleffective overburden) CRR;s = Resisting Cyclic Stress Ratio. It is a function of the content of fines
The induced cyclic stress ratio (CSR 1) by a given peak ground acceleration (acc. max) is: (Figure 2, NCEER Workshop, after Seed et al 1985))
CSR i = 0.65 x ace max. x total normal stress x rd / {effective normal stress x g) Factor of Safety, F, is:
F, = CRRr/CSRi

Liquefaction analysis is performed fallowing Seed's Procedure, outlined by Seed et ai, (7985),
Seed and Harder (1990), updated NCEER (1997}, Martin et ai. (1999) and Youd et al. (2001) The resisting cyclic stress ratio {CRR r) is equal to:
CRR r= CRR;5 x Gm x K sigma
Emear/E60 = Energy Ratio to correct to standard 60% Energy
Surcharge = Any surcharge on top of the ground {psf} where: Gm = Earthquake Magnitude Correction Factor (Glake, NCEER Table 3, Eq.4}
ace. max = maximum peak acceleration at the ground surface (g's) K sigma = Normalized resistance factor (Fig. 14 from NCEER Liquefaction workshop, Harder Boulanger)
1Oy = square root of (100 kPa or 1.04427 tsfleffective overburden) CRRys = Resisting Cyclic Stress Ratio. It is a function of the contert of fines
The induced cycfic stress satic (CSR i) by a given peak ground acceleration (acc. max) is: (Figure 2, NCEER Workshop, after Seed et al 1985}}
CSR i = 0,66 x acc max. x total normal stress x rd / {effective normal stress x g) Factor of Safety, F, is:
FL= ORRr/CSRI

Kapatama Container Wharf Project
LIQUEFACTION ANALYSIS (CLE}, M = 7 ; ACC. = 0.182 g
Liquefaction analysis is performed following Seed's Procedure, outlined by Seed et al. (1985),
Seed and Harder (1890), updated NCEER (1997), Martin st af. (1999) and Youd et af. (2001)
EmearvE60 = Energy Ratio to correct to standard 60% Energy
Surcharge = Any surcharge on top of the ground (psf)
acc, max = maximum peak acceleration at the ground surface (g's)
1Oy = square root of (100 kPa or 1.04427 teffeffective overburden)
The induced cyclic stress ratio (CSR i) by a given peak ground acceleration (ace. max) is:
CSR i = 0.65 x ace max. x total normal sess x rd / (affective normal stress x g}
EMI Project No.
The resisting cyclic stress ratio (CRR r) is equal to:
CRRr=CRR,;5xCmxK sigma
where: Cm = Earthquake Magnitude Correction Factor (Blake, NCEER Table 3, Eq.4)
K sigma = Normalized resistance factor (Fig. 14 from NCEER Liquefaction workshop, Harder Boulanger}
CRRr.s = Resisting Cyclic Stress Ratio. It is a function of the content of fines
(Figure 2, NCEER Workshop, after Seed et a! 1985))
Factor of Safety, F, is:
F,= CRRr/CSRi

LIQUEFACTION ANALYSIS (CLE), M= 7; ACC.=0.182 g
Liquefaction analysis is performed following Seed's Procedure, outlined by Seed et al. (1985),
Seed and Harder (1990), updated NCEER (1997), Marti ef al. (4999) and Youd et al. (2004) The resisting cyclic stress ratio {CRR r) Is equal to:
CRR r= CRRr6 x Cm x K sigme
EmearvE60 = Energy Ratio to correct to standard 60% Energy
Surcharge = Any surcharge on top of the ground (ps?) where: Cm = Earthquake Magnitude Correction Factor (Blake, NCEER Table 3, Eq.4)
ace. max = maximum peak acceleration at the ground surface (g's) K sigma = Normalized resistance factor (Fig. 14 from NCEER Liquefaction workshop, Harder Boulanger)
‘Cy = square root of (100 kPa or 1.04427 tstleffective overburden) CRR;;5 = Resisting Cyclic Stress Ratio. It is a function of the content of fines
The induced cyclic stress ratio (CSR i} by a given peak ground acceleration (acc. max) is: (Figure 2, NCEER Workshop, after Seed et at 1885})
CSR i = 0.65 x ace max. x lotal normal stress x rd / (effective normal stress x g) Factor of Safety, F;, is:
Fp = CRRr?CSRI

LIQUEFACTION ANALYSIS (CLE), M=7; ACC, = 0.182 g
Liquefaction analysis is performed following Seed's Procedure, outlined by Seed et al. (1985),
Seed and Harder (1990), updated NCEER (1997), Martin et al. (1999) and Youd et al. (2001)
EmearvE60 = Energy Ratio to correct to standard 60% Energy
Surcharge = Any surcharge on top of the ground {psf}
acc, max = maximum peak acceleration at tha ground surface (g's)
‘Cy = square root of (100 kPa or 1.04427 tsffeffective overburden)
The induced cyclic stress ratio (CSR #} by a given peak ground acceleration (acc. max) is:
CSR i= 0.65 x acc max. x total normal stress x rd / (effective normal stress x g)

EMI Project No. 13-104
Version ~ 12/05,
The resisting cyclic stress ratio (CRR r) is equal to:
CRR r= CRR;5x Gm x K sigma
where: Cm = Earthquake Magnitude Correction Factor (Blake, NCEER Table 3, Eq.4)
K sigma = Normalized resistance factor (Fig. 14 from NCEER Liquefaction workshop, Harder Boulanger)
CRR;,; = Resisting Cyclic Stress Ratio. It is a function of the content of fines
(Figure 2, NCEER Workshop, after Seed at al 1985))
Factor of Safety, F, is:
FL= ORR t?CSRi

= CRRr/CSRi

LIQUEFACTION ANALYSIS (CLE), M= 7; ACC. =0.182g
Liquefaction analysis is performed foflowing Seed’s Pracedure, outlined by Sead at al. (1985),
Seed and Harder (1990), updated NCEER (1997), Martin ef a!. (1999) and Youd ef al. (2001)
EmeantE6¢ = Energy Ratio to correct to standard 60% Energy
Surcharge = Any surcharge on top of the ground (psf}
ace, Max = maximum peak acceleration at the ground surface (9's)
‘Cy = square root of (400 kPa or 1.04427 tstleffective overburden)
The induced cyclic stress ratio (CSR i) by a given peak ground acceleration (acc, max) is:
CSR I= 0,65 x ace max. x total normal stress x rd / (effective normal stress x g)

The resisting cyclic stress ratio (CRR r) is equal to:
GRR r= CRR, sx Cmx K sigma
where: Gm = Earthquake Magnitude Correction Factor (Blake, NCEER Table 3, Eq.4)
K sigma = Normafized resistance factor (Fig. 14 from NCEER Liquefaction workshop, Harder Boulanger)
CRR;-s = Resisting Cyclic Stress Ratio. It is a function of the content of fines
{Figure 2, NCEER Workshop, after Seed et al 1985))
Factor of Safety, F, is:
FL= CRRr/CSRI

LIQUEFACTION ANALYSIS (CLE), M= 7; ACC. = 0.182 g
Liquefaction analysis is performed follawing Seed's Procedure, outlined by Seed et al. (1985),
Seed and Harder (1990}, updated NCEER (1997), Martin et af. (1999) and Youd et al. (2001) The resisting cyctic stress ratio (CRR r) is aqual to:
CRR t= CRR;5x Cm x K sigma
Emean/E60 = Energy Ratio to correct to standard 60% Energy
Surcharge = Any surcharge on top of the ground (psf) where: Cm = Earthquake Magnitude Correction Factor (Blake, NCEER Table 3, Eq.4)
acc. max = maximum peak acceleration at the ground surface (g's) K sigma = Normalized resistance factor (Fig. 14 fom NCEER Liquefaction workshop, Harder Boulanger)
1Cy = square root of (100 kPa or 1.04427 tsfeffective overburden) CRR;,s = Resisting Cychic Stress Ratio. It is a function of the content of fines
The induced cyclic stress ratio (CSR i) by a given peak graund acceleration (acc. max) is: (Figure 2, NCEER Workshop, after Seed et al 1985))
CSR t = 0.65 x acc max. x total normal stress x rd / (effective normal stress x g) Factor of Safety, F;, is:
F, = CRRr/CSRi

Kapalama Contalner Wharf Project EM] Project No, 13-104 Version - 12/05
LIQUEFACTION ANALYSIS {CLE), M=7; ACC. =0.182 g
Liquefaction analysis is performed following Seed's Procedure, outlined by Seed et al. (7985),
Seed and Harder (1990}, updated NCEER (1997), Martin et al. (1999) and Youd et at. (2001) The resisting cyclic stress ratio (CRR r) is equal to:
CRR r= CRR,, x Cm x K sigma
EmearvE60 = Energy Ratio to correct to standard 60% Energy
Surcharge = Any surcharge on top of the ground (psf) where: Cm = Earthquake Magnitude Correction Factor (Blake, NCEER Table 3, £q.4)
acc. max = maximum peak acceleration at the ground surface (g’s} K sigma = Normalized resistance factor (Fig. 14 from NCEER Liquefaction workshop, Harder Boulanger}
‘Cy = square root of (100 kPa or 1.04427 tsffeffective overburden) CRR;,5 = Resisting Gyciic Stress Ratio. It is a function of the content of fines
The induced cyclic stress ratio (CSR |) by a given peak ground acceleration (acc. max) is: (Figure 2, NCEER Workshop, after Seed of al 1985))
CSR i = 0.65 x acc max. x total normal stress x 1d / (eflective normal siress x g) Factor of Safety, F, is:
F,= CRRr/CSRI

Kapatama Container Wharf Project
LIQUEFACTION ANALYSIS (CLE), M= 7; ACC. = 0.182g
Liquefaction analysis is performed folowing Seed’s Procedure, outlined by Seed et al. (1985),
Seed and Harder (1990), updated NCEER (1997), Martin et al. (1999) and Youd et al. (2001)
Emean/E60 = Energy Ratio fo correct to standard 60% Energy
Surcharge = Any surcharge on top of the ground (psi}
acc. max = maximum peak acceleration at the groumd surface (g's)
"Cy = square root of (100 kPa or 1.04427 tst/effactive overburden)
The induced cyclic stress ratio (CSR jf) by a given peak ground acceleration (acc. max) is:
CSR i= 0.65 x acc max. x total normal stress x rd / {effective normal stress x g)
The resisting cyclic stress ratlo (CRR r) is equal ta:
CRR c= CRR7s5x Cm x K sigma
where: Cm = Earthquake Magnilude Correction Factor (Blake, NCEER Table 3, Eq.4)
K sigma = Normatized resistance factor (Fig. 14 from NCEER Liquefaction workshop, Harder Boulanger}
CRR;5» = Resisting Cyclic Stress Ratio. It is a function of the content of fines
(Figure 2, NCEER Workshop, after Seed et at 1985))
Factor of Safety, F, is:
FL=CRRI/CSRi ~

Liquefaction analysts is performed following Seed’s Procedure, outlined by Seed et al. (1985),
Seed and Harder (1990), updated NCEER (1997), Martin at al. (1999) and Youd et al. (2004) The resisting cyclic stress ratio (CRR 1) is equal to:
CRR r= CRR;s5 x Cm x K sigma
EmeanE60 = Energy Ratio to correct to standard 60% Energy
Surcharge = Any surcharge on tep of the ground (psf}
acc. max = maximum peak acceleration at the ground surface (9’s)
‘Cy = square root of (100 kPa or 1.04427 tsffeffective overburden)
The induced cyclic stress ratio (CSR #} by a given peak ground acceleration (acc. max) is:
where: Gm = Earthquake Magnitude Correction Factor (Blake, NCEER Table 3, Eq.4)
{ sigma = Normalized resistance factor (Fig. 14 from NCEER Liquefaction workshop, Harder Boulanger)
CRR;5 = Resisting Cyclic Stress Ratio. It is a function of the content of fines
(Figure 2, NCEER Workshop, after Sead et al 1985)}
CSR i = 0.65 x ace max. x total normal stress x rd / (effective normal stress x g) Factor of Safety, F,, is:
FL= CRRr/CsRi

Liquefaction analysis Is performed following Seed's Procedure, outlined by Seed et al. (1985),
Sead and Harder (1990), updated NCEER (1997), Martin et al. (1999) and Youd et al. (2001) The resisting cyclic stress ratio (CRR r) is equal to:
CRR t= CRR7s5 x Gm x K sigma
£meanvE60 = Energy Ratio to correct to standard 60% Energy
Surcharge = Any surcharge on top of the ground (psf) where: Gm = Earthquake Magnitude Carrection Factor (Blake, NCEER Yable 3, Eg.4)
acc. max = maximum peak acceleration at the ground surface (9's) K sigma = Nermatized resistance factor (Fig. 14 from NCEER Liquefaction workshop, Harder Boulanger)
‘Gy = square root of (100 kPa or 1.04427 tsfleffective overburden} GRR; = Resisting Cyclic Stress Ratio, It is a funotion of the cantent of fines
The induced cyclic stress ratio (CSR i} by a given peak ground acceleration (acc. max) is: (Figure 2, NCEER Workshop, after Seed et al 1985))
CSR i = 0.65 x acc max. x total normal stress x ed / {effective normal stress x g) Factor of Safety, F, is:
FL = CRRr/CSRi

LIQUEFACTION ANALYSIS (DE), M =7 : ACC.-= 0.255 g
Liquefaction analysis is performed following Seeds Procedure, outlined by Sead et al. (1985},
Seed and Harder (1990), updated NCEER (1997), Martin et al. (1999) and Youd ef al. (2001)
Emean/E60 = Energy Ratio to correct to standard 60% Energy
Surcharge = Any surcharge on top of the ground (psf)
acc. max = maximum peak acceleration at the graund surface (g's)
‘Cy = square root of (100 kPa or 1.04427 tsffeffective overburden)
The induced cyclic stress ratio (CSR i) by a given peak ground acceleration (acc. max} is:
CSR | = 0.65 x ace max. x total normat stress x rd / (effective normal stress x 9)
EMI Project No. 13104
Version - 12405
wan ee
The resisting cyclic stress ratio (CRR r) is equal to:
CRR r= CRR;5x Cm x K sigma
where: Cm = Earthquake Magnitude Correction Factor (Blake, NCEER Table 3, &q.4)
K sigma = Normalized resistance factor (Fig. 14 from NCEER Liquefaction workshop, Harder Boulanger)
CRRz,s = Resisting Cyclic Stress Ratio. It is a function of the content of fines
(Figure 2, NCEER Workshop, after Seed et al 1985))
Factor of Safety, F, is:
F,= CRRr/CSRi

cemanammacnans samanaane seuReadddmmterrrerem—r rs ere eatin, <<a, SADdQQG@Ge@GGG0@@GG2Q@QGCneee tire QQ CCC EO NTT TTCEE REECE ARRAN NE RRRRRRRRR ERR RRRRE ERP NY meeenenenne Renn reews werreremmeeentirre eemecenmmenentey dntnitteeeencne

Liquefaction anatysis is performed following Seed's Procedure, outlined by Seed et al. (1985),
Seed and Harder (1990), updated NCEER (1997), Martin et al. (7999) and Youd et al. (2001) The resisting cyclic stress ratio (CRR r) is equat to:
CRR r = CRR;,; x Gm x K sigma
Emear/E60 = Energy Ratio to correct to standard 60% Energy
Surcharge = Any surcharge on top of the ground (psf) where:
acc. max = maximum peak acceleration at the ground surface {9's}
‘Cx = square root of (100 kPa or 1.04427 teffeffective overburden)
The induced cyclic stress ratio (CSR i) by a given peak ground acceleration (acc. max) is:
Cm = Earthquake Magnitude Correction Factor (Blake, NCEER Table 3, Eq.4)
K sigma = Normalized resistance factor (Fig. 14 from NCEER Liquefaction workshop, Harder Boulanger)
CRR; = Resisting Cyclic Stress Ratio. It is a function of the content of fines
(Figure 2, NCEER Workshop, alter Seed et al 1985))
CSR i = 0.65 x acc max. x total normal stress x rd / {effective normal stress x g) Factar of Safety, F, is:
FL = CRRr/CSRi

LIQUEFACTION ANALYSIS (DE), M= 7; ACC. = 0.255 g
Liquefaction analysis is performed following Seed's Procedure, outlined by Seed et al. (7985),
Seed and Harder (1990), updated NCEER (1997), Martin et a?. (1999) and Youd et at. (2001) The resisting cyclic stress ratio (CRR 1) is equal to:
GRR r= CRR;s x Cmx K sigma
Emean/E60 = Energy Ratio to correct to standard 60% Energy
Surcharge = Any surcharge on top of the ground (psf) where: Cm = Earthquake Magnitude Correction Factor (Glake, NCEER Table 3, Eq.4)
acc, max = maximum peak acceleration at the ground surface (q's) K sigma = Normalized resistance factor (Fig. 14 from NCEER Liquefaction workshop, Harder Boulanger)
‘Cy = square root of (100 kPa or 4.04427 tsfieifeative overburden) CRR;.5 = Resisting Cyclic Stress Ratio. It is a function of the content of fines
The Induced cyclic stress ratio (CSR I) by a given peak ground acceleration (acc. max) ts: (Figure 2, NCEER Workshop, after Seed et al 1985))
CSR i = 0.85 x ace max, x total normal stress x rd / (effective normal stress x 9) Factor of Safety, F, is:
FL= CRRr/CSRi

LIQUEFACTION ANALYSIS (DE), M= 7; ACC, = 0.255 g
Liquefaction analysis ts performed following Seed's Procedure, outlined by Seed et al. (1985),
Seed and Harder (1990), updated NCEER (1997}, Martin et al. (1999) and Youd et al. (2001)
Emean/E60 = Energy Ratio to correct to standard 60% Energy
Surcharge = Any surcharge on top of the ground (psf)
26C. Max = Maximum peak acceleration at the ground surface (g's)
‘Cy = square root of (100 kPa or 1.04427 tsfeffective overburden)
The induced cyclic stress ratia (CSR i) by a given peak ground acceleration (ace. max) is:
CSR | = 0.65 x ace max. x tolal normal stress x rd / (effective normat stress x g)

The resisting cyclic stress ratio (CRR r) is equal to:
GRR & = CRR,5x Cm x K sigma
where: Cm = Earthquake Magnitude Correction Factor (Blake, NCEER Table 3, Eq.4)
K sigma = Normatized resistance factor (Fig, 14 from NCEER Liquefaction workshop, Harder Boulanger)
GRR;,s5 = Resisting Cyctic Stress Ratio, It is a function of the content of fines
(Figure 2, NCEER Workshop, after Seed et at 1985))
Factor of Safety, F, is:
FL = CRRr/CSRi

Kapalama Container Wharf Project
LIQUEFACTION ANALYSIS (DE), M = 7; ACC. = 0.2559
it nee
Liquefaction analysis is performed following Ssed’s Procedure, outlined by Seed et al. (1985),
Seed and Harder (1990), updated NCEER (1997), Martin et al. (1999) and Youd et al. (2001)
EmearvE60 = Energy Ratio to correct to standard 60% Energy
Surcharge = Any surcharge on top of the ground (psf)
acc. max = maximum peak acceleration af the ground surface (g's)
‘Cy = square root of (100 kPa or 1.04427 taifeffective overburden)
The induced cyclic stress ratio (CSR |) by a given peak ground acceleration (acc. max) Is:
CSR i = 0.65 x ace max. x total normal stress x rd / (effective normat stress x g)
EMI Project No. 13-104
The resisting cyclic stress ratio (CRR r) Is equal to:
CRR r= CRR75 x Cm x K sigma
where: Cm = Earthquake Magnitude Carrection Factor (Blake, NCEER Table 3, Eq.4)
K sigma = Normalized resistance factor (Fig. 14 from NCEER Liquefaction workshop, Harder Boulanger)
CRR;,s = Resisting Cyclic Stress Ratio. It is a function of the content of fines
(Figure 2, NCEER Workshop, after Seed et al 1985))
Factor of Safety, F;, is:
F,= CRRr/CSRi

Liquefaction analysis Is performed following Seed's Procedure, ottlinad by Seed et al. (1985),
Seed and Harder (1890}, updated NCEER (1997), Martin et al, (1999) and Youd et al. (2004} The resisting cyclic stress ratio (CRR r) is equal to:
CRR r= CRR;s x Cm x sigma
EmeanvE60 = Energy Ratio to correct to standard 60% Energy
Surcharge = Any surcharge on top of the ground (psf) where: = Cm = Earthquake Magnitude Correction Factor (Blake, NCEER Table 3, Eq.4)
acc, max = maximum peak acceleration at the ground surface (g's} K sigma = Normalized resistance factor (Fig. 14 from NCEER Liquefaction workshop, Harder Boulanger)
‘yy = square root of (100 kPa or 1.04427 tefleffective overburden) CRR;. = Resisting Cyclic Stress Ratio. It is a function of the content of fines
The induced cyclic stress ratio (CSR i) by a given peak ground acceleration (acc. max) is: (Figure 2, NCEER Workshop, after Seed et al 1985)}
CSR i = 0.65 x ace max. x total normal stress x 1d / (effective normal stress x 9) Factor of Safety, F, is:
FL= CGRRr/CSRi

LIQUEFACTION ANALYSIS (DE), M = 7; ACC. = 0.255
Liquefaction analysis is performed fofowiny Seed’s Procedure, outlined by Seed et al. (1985),
Seed and Harder (1990), updated NCEER (1997), Martin et al. (1999) and Youd et al. (2001)
EmearvE60 = Energy Ratio to correct to standard 60% Energy
Surcharge > Any surcharge on top of the ground (psf)
acc. max = maximum peak acceleration at ihe ground surface (g's)
‘Cy = square root of {100 kPa or 1.04427 tst/effective overburden)
The induced cyclic stress ratio (CSR i} by a given peak ground acceleration (acc. max) is:
CSR i = 0.65 x ace max. x total normal stress x rd / (effective normal stress x g)
EM Project No.
The resisting cycllc stress ratio (CRR +) is equal to:
CRR r= GRR. x Cm x K sigma
where:
13-104
Cm = Earthquake Magnitude Correction Factor (Blake, NCEER Table 3, Eq.4)
Version - 12/05
K sigma = Normafized resistance factor (Fig. 14 from NCEER Liquefaction workshop, Harder Boulanger)
CRR;, = Resisting Cyclic Stress Ratio. It is a function of the content of fines
(Figure 2, NCEER Workshop, after Sead et al 1985))
Factor af Safety, F, is:
Does not fiquefy per Boutanger and idriss (2008) and Bray and Sencio (2008)
F,= CRRr/CSRi

LIQUEFACTION ANALYSIS (DE), M=7 ; ACC..= 0.255 g
Liquefaction anatysis is performed folkawing Sead’s Procedure, outlined by Seed et al, (1985),
Seed and Harder (1990}, updatad NCEER (7997), Martini et al, (1999) and Youd et a!. (2001)
EmearvEs0 = Energy Ratio to correct to standard 60% Energy
Surcharge = Any surcharge on top of the ground (psf)
acc. max = maximum peak acceleration at the ground surface (g's}
‘Cy = square root of (100 kPa or 1.04427 tsffeffective overburden)
The induced cyclic stress ratio (CSR i) by a given peak ground acceleration (ace, max) is:
CSR i= 0.65 x acc max. x total normal stress x rd / (effective normal stress x g)
EMI Project No, 13-104
Version - 12/05
The resisting cyclic stress ratio (CRR r) is equal to:
CRR r= CRR,5x Cm x K sigma
where: Cm = Earthquake Magnitude Correction Factor (Blake, NCEER Table 3, Eq.4}
K sigma = Normalized resistance factor (Fig. 14 ifom NCEER Liquefaction workshop, Harder Boulanger)
CRR;5 = Resisting Cyclic Stress Ratlo. It is a function of the cantent of fines
(Figure 2, NCEER Workshop, after Seed et a! 1985)
Factor of Safety, F, is:
F,= CRRe/CSRi

Seed and Harder (1990), undated NCEER (1997), Martin et al. (4999) and Youd et al, (2001)
Emean/E60 = Energy Ratie to correct to standard 60% Energy
Surcharge = Any surcharge on top of the ground (psf)
ace. max = maximum peak acceleration at the ground surface (g's)
‘Cy = square root of (100 kPa or 1.04427 tsffeffective overburden)
The induced cyclic stress ratio (CSR i) by a given peak ground acceleration (gcc. max} is:
CSR i = 0.65 x ace max. x total normal stress x rd / (effective normal stress x g)
The resisting cyclic stress ratio (CRR r) is equal to:
CRR r= GRR;sx Gm x K sigma
where: Cm = Earthquake Magnitude Correction Factor (Blake, NCEER Table 3, Eq.4}
K sigma = Normalized resistance factor (Fig. 14 from NCEER Liquefaction workshop, Harder Boulanger)
CRR;.s = Resisting Cyclic Stress Ratio. It is a function of the content of fines
(Figure 2, NCEER Workshop, after Seed et ai 1985))
Factor of Safety, F, is:
F,= CRRr/CSRiI

Liquefaction analysis is performed following Seed's Procedure, outlined by Seed et af. (1985),
Seed and Harder (1990), updated NCEER (1997), Martin et al. (1999) and Youd et al. (2001) The resisting cyclic stress ratio (CRR r) is equal to:
CRR r= CRRy5 x Cm x K sigma
EmearvE60 = Energy Ratio to correct to standard 60% Energy
Surcharge = Any surcharge on top of the ground (psf) where: Cm = Earthquake Magnitude Correction Factor (Blake, NCEER Table 3, Eq.4}
acc. max = maximum peak acceleration at the ground surface (9's) K sigma = Normalized resistance factor (Fig. 14 from NCEER Liquefaction workshop, Harder Boutanger)
"Cu = square root of (100 kPa or 1.04427 tsi/effactive overburden) CRR;.5 = Resisting Cyclic Stress Ratio. It is a function of the content of fines
The induced cyclic stress ratio (CSR i) by a given peak ground acceleration (acc. max} is: (Figure 2, NCEER Workshop, after Seed et al 1985))
CSR i = 0.65 x ace max. x total normal stress x rd / (effective normal siress x g) Factor of Safety, F,, is:
F,= CRRr/CSRi

Liquefaction analysis is performed following Seed's Procedvre, outlined by Seed et al. (1985),
Seed and Harder (1890), updated NCEER (1997), Martin et af. (1999) and Youd et af. (2001}
Emesn/E60 = Energy Ratio to corract to standard 60% Energy
Surcharge = Any surcharge on top of the ground {psf}
acc. max = maximum peak acceleration at the ground surface (g's)
‘Cy = square roat of (100 kPa or 1.04427 tsffeffective overburden)
The induced cyclic stress ratio {CSR |) by a given peak ground acceleration (acc. max) is:
CSR i = 0.65 x acc max. x total normal stress x rd / (effective normal stress x g)
EMI Project No.
The resisting cyclic stress ratio (CRR r) is equal to:
CRR r= CRR?z» x Cm x K sigma
where: Cm = Earhquake Magnitude Correction Factor (Blake, NCEER Table 3, Eq.4)
K sigma = Normalized resistance factor (Fig. 14 fram NCEER Liquefaction workshop, Harder Boulanger}
CRR;,5 = Resisting Cyclic Streas Ratio. ttis a function of the content of fines
{Figure 2, NCEER Workshop, after Sead et al 1985))
Factor of Safety, F;, is:
F, = CRRr/CSRi

LIQUEFACTION ANALYSIS (DE), M = 7 ; ACC..=0.255q
Liquefaction analysis is performed faliawing Seed's Procedure, cutlined by Seed et all. (4985),
Seed and Harder (1990), updated NCEER (1997), Martin et al. (1999) and Youd et al. (2001) The resisting cyclic stress ratio (CRR r) is equal to:
CRR r= CRR;s x Cm x K sigma
Emean/E60 = Energy Ratio to correct to standard 60% Energy
Surcharge = Any surcharge on top of the ground (psf) where: Cm = Earthquake Magnitude Correction Factor (Blake, NCEER Tabie 3, Eq.4)
acc. max = maximum peak acceleration at the ground surface (g's) K sigma = Normalized resistance factor (Fig. 14 from NCEER Liquefaction workshop, Harder Boulanger)
‘Cy = square root of (100 kPa or 1.04427 tsffeffective overburden) CRR?5 = Resisting Cyclic Stress Ratio. It is a function of the content of fines
The induced cyclic stress ratio (CSR 1} by a given peak ground acceleration (ace. max) is: (Figure 2, NCEER Workshop, after Seed et al 1985))
CSR i = 0.65 x acc max. x tolal normal stress x rd / (effective normal stress x g) Factor of Safety, F, Is:
Fp = CRRe/CSRi

LIQUEFACTION ANALYSIS (MCE), M= 7.5; ACC=0.359 g
Liquefaction analysis is performed following Seed’s Procedure, outlined by Seed et at. (1985),
Seed and Harder (1990), updated NCEER (1997), Martin et al. (1999) and Youd et al. (2004) The resisting cyclic stress ratio (CRR r) is equal to:
CRR f= GRR; x Cm x K sigma
EmearvE60 = Energy Ratio to correct to standard 60% Energy
Surcharge = Any surcharge on top of the ground (nsf} where: Cm = Earthquake Magnitude Correction Factor (Blake, NCEER Table 3, Eq.4)
aoc. max = maximum peak acceleration at the ground surface (g's) K sigma = Normalized resistance factor (Fig. 14 from NCEER Liquefaction workshop, Harder Saulanger)
‘Cy = Square root of (100 kPa or 1.04427 tsffeffective overburden} CRR;,5 = Resisting Cyctic Stress Ratio. It is a function of the content of fines
The induced cyclic stress ratio (CSR § by a given peak ground acceleration (acc. max) is: (Figure 2, NCEER Workshop, after Seed et al 1985))
CSR i = 0.65 x acc max. x total normal stress x rd / (effective normal stress x g) Factor of Safety, F;, is:
FL= CRRr/CSRi

Liquefaction analysis is performed following Seed's Procedure, outlined by Seed et al. (1985),
Seed and Harder (1990), updated NCEER (1997), Martin et al. (1999) arxt Youd et al. (2007) The resisting cyclic stress ralio {CRR 1) Is equal to:
CRRr= CRR;, x Cm x K sigme
Emean/E60 = Energy Ratio to correct to standard 60% Energy
Surcharge = Any surcharge on top of the ground (psf} where: Cm = Earthquake Magnitude Correction Factor (Blake, NCEER Table 3, Eq.4)
acc. max = maximum peak acceteration at the ground surface (9's) K sigma = Normatized resistance factor (Fig. 14 from NCEER Liquefaction workshop, Harder Boulanger)
‘Cy = Square root of (100 kPa or 1.04427 tsffeffective overburden} CRR;.5 = Resisting Cyclic Stress Ratio. It is a function of the content of fines
The induced cyclic stress ratio (CSR i) by a given peak ground acceleration (acc, max) is: (Figure 2, NCEER Workshop, after Seed et al 1985))
CSR 1 = 0,65 x acc max. x totat normal stress x rd / (effective normal stress x g) Factor of Safety, F., is:
F, = CRRr/CSRi

LIQUEFACTION ANALYSIS (MCE), M = 7.5 7 ACC,-= 0.359.g
Liquefaction analysis is performed folowing Seed’s Procedure, outlined by Seed et al. (1985),
Seed and Harder (1990), updated NCEER (1997), Martin et al. (1999) and Youd et ai. (2001) The resisting cyclic stress ratio (CRR t) is equal to:
CRR r= CRR;, x Gm x sigma
Emear/E60 = Energy Ratio to correct to standard 60% Energy
Surcharge = Any surcharge on top of the ground {psf} where: Cm = Earthquake Magnitude Correction Factor (Blake, NCEER Table 3, Eq.4)
acc, max = maximum peak acceleration at the ground surface (g's) K sigma = Nomnmalized resistance factor (Fig. 14 from NCEER Liquefaction workshop, Harder Boutanger)
Cx = square root of (100 kPa or 1.64427 teffeffective overburden) CRR;s = Resisting Cyclic Stress Ratio. It is a function of the content of fines
The ingkiced cyclic stress ratio (CSR 1) by a given peak ground acceleration (acc. max) is: (Figure 2, NCEER Workshop, after Seed et al 1985)
GSR i= 0.65 x acc max. x totat normal stress x rd / (effective normal stress x g) Factor of Safety, F, is:
FL = CRRr/CSRi

‘Cy = square rool of (100 kPa or 1.04427 tsffeffective overburden) CRR;, = Resisting Cyclic Stress Ratio, itis a function of the content of fines

Kapalama Container Wharf Project EM! Project No. 13-104 Version - 1205
LIQUEFACTION ANALYSIS (MCE), M = 7.5 ;ACC.= 0.359 g
Liuefaction anatysis is performed following Seed’s Procedure, outlined by Seed et al. (1985),
Seed and Harder (1990), updated NCEER (1997), Martin et af. (1999) and Youd et af. (2001) The resisting cyclic stress ratia (CRR r) is equal to:
CRR r= GRR;,s5 x Cm x K sigma
Emean/E60 = Energy Ratio to correct to standard 60% Energy
Surcharge = Any surcharge on top of the ground (psf) where: Om = Earthquake Magnitude Correction Factor (Blake, NCEER Table 3, Eq.4)
acc. max = maximum peak acceleration at the ground surface (9's} K sigma = Normalized resistance factor (Fig. 14 from NCEER Liquefaction workshop, Harder Boulanger)
‘Cy = square roat of (100 kPa or 1.04427 tstfeffective overburden) ORR; = Resisting Cyclic Stress Ratio. It Is a function of the content of fines
The induced cyclic stress ratio (CSR i) by a given peak ground acceleration (acc. max) is: (Figure 2, NCEER Workshop, after Seed at al 1985))
CSR i = 0.65 x ace max, x total normal stress x sd / (eflective normal stress x g) Factor of Safety, F, is:
F, = CRRr/CSRi

LIQUEFACTION ANALYSIS (MCE), M= 7.5; ACC. =0.359g
Liquefaction analysis is performed following Seed's Procedura, outlined by Seed et al. (1985),
Seed and Harder (1990), updated NCEER (1997), Martin et al. (1999) and Youd et al. (2001) The rasisting cyclic siress ratio (GRR 1) is equal to:
CRR r= CRR75x Cm x K sigma
Emean/E60 = Energy Ratio to correct to standard 60% Energy
Surcharge = Any surcharge on top of the ground (psf} where: Cm = £arthquake Magnitude Correction Factor (Blake, NCEER Table 3, Eq.4)
acc. max = maximum peak acceleration at the ground surface {g's} K sigma = Normalized resistance factor (Fig. 14 from NCEER Liquefaction workshop, Harder Boulanger)
'Cy = square root of (100 kPa or 1.04427 tsffeffective overburden) CRR;5 = Resisting Cyclic Stress Ratio. It s a function of the content of fines
The induced cyclic stress ratio (CSR i) by a given peak ground acceleration (acc. max) is: (Figure 2, NCEER Workshop, after Seed et al 1985)}
CSR i = 0.65 x acc max. x total normal stress x rd / (effective normal stress x g) Factor of Safety, F, Is:
F,= CRRrfCSRi

LIQUEFACTION ANALYSIS (MCE), M = 7.5: ACC: = 0.359. g
Liquefaction analysis is performed following Seed's Procedure, outlined by Seed et al. (1985),
Seed and Harder (1990), updated NCEER (1997), Martin et al. (1999) and Youd et ai. (2001) The resisting cyclic stress ratio (CRR r) Is equal to:
CRE f= CRR,s x Cm x K sigma
EmeanE60 = Energy Ratio te correct to standard 60% Energy
Surcharge = Any surcharge on top of the ground (psf) where: Cm = Earthquake Magnitude Correction Factor (Blake, NCEER Table 3, Eq.4}
acc, max = maxinvum peak acceleration at the ground surface (g's) i sigma = Normalized resistance factor (Fig. 14 from NCEER Liquefaction workshop, Harder Boulanger)
‘Cy = square root of (100 kPa or 1.04427 tsffeffective overburden) CRR;5 = Resisting Cyclic Stress Ratio, !f is a function of the content of fmes
The induced cyclic stress ratio (CSR i) by a given peak ground acceleration {acc. max) is: (Figure 2, NCEER Workshop, afier Seed et al 1935)}
CSR i = 0.65 x acc max. x total normal stress x rd / (effective normal stress x g) Factor of Safety, F, is:
Fi = CRRr/cSRi

LIQUEFACTION ANALYSIS (MCE), M = 7.5; ACC. = 0,359 g
Liquefaction analysis is performed following Seed's Procedure, outined by Seed et al. (1985),
Seed and Harder (1990), updated NCEER (1997), Martin at af. (1999) and Youd ef al. (2001) The resisting cyclic stress ratio (CRR 1) s equal to:
CRR r= CRR;sx Cm x K sigma
Emearv/E60 = Energy Ratio to correct to standard 60% Energy
Surcharge = Any surcharge on top of the ground (psf) where: Cm = Earthquake Magnitude Correction Factor (Blake, NCEER Table 3, £q.4)
acc. max = maximum peak acceleration at the ground surface (g's) « sigma = Normalized resistance factor (Fig. 14 from NCEER tiquefaction workshop, Harder Boulanger)
‘Cy = square root of (100 kPa or 1.04427 tsffeffective overburden) CRR;.5 = Resisting Cyctic Stress Ratio. It is a function of the content of fines
The induced cyclic stress ratio (CSR i) by a given peak graund acceleration (aca. max) Is: (Figure 2, NCEER Workshop, after Seed et al 1995)
CSR i = 0.65 x ace max. x tofal normat stress x rd / (effective norma stress x g) Factor of Safety, F,, is:
FL = CRRr/CSRi

CRR r= CRR;5 x Cm x K sigma
EmearvE60 = Energy Ratio to correct to standard 60% Energy
Surcharge = Any surcharge on top of the ground (psf} where: Cm = Earthquake Magnitude Correction Factor (Blake, NCEER Table 3, Eq.4)
acc. max = maximum peak acceleration at the ground surface (g's} K sigma = Normalized resistance factor (Fig. 14 from NCEER Liquefaction workshop, Harder Boulanger)
1c, = square root of (100 kPa or 1.0442? isffeffective overburden) CRR;,s = Resisting Cyclic Stress Ratio. !t is a function of the content of fines
The induced cyclic stress ratio (CSR i) by a given peak graund acceleration (acc. max) is: {Figure 2, NCEER Workshop, after Seed et al 1965)
CSR t = 0.65 x aoc max, x total norma} stress x rd / (effective normal stress x 9) Factor of Safety, Fy is:

Cm = Earthquake Magnitude Correction Factor (Blake, NCEER Table 3, Eq.4)
K sigma = Normalized resistance factor (Fig, 14 from NCEER Liquefaction workshop, Harder Boulanger}
CRR;.s5 = Resisting Cyclic Stress Ratio. It is a function of the content of fines
(Figure 2, NCEER Workshop, after Seec et at 1985)

Surcharge = Any surcharge on top of the ground (psf)
acc. max = maximum peak acceleration at the ground surface (g's)
‘Cy = square root of (106 kPa or 1.04427 tsffeffective overburden)
The induced cyclic stress ratio (CSR } by a given peak ground acceleration (ace. max) is:
CSR i = 0.65 x acc max. x total normal stress x rd / (effective normal stress x g)
EMI Project No. 13-104 Version - 12/08
The resisting cyclic stress ratio (CRR r) is equal to:
CRR r= CRR;5x Cm x K sigma
where: Cm = Earthquake Magnitude Correction Factor (Blake, NCEER Table 3, Eq.4)
K sigma = Normalized resistance factor (Fig. 14 from NCEER Liquefaction workshop, Harder Boulanger}
CRRz,s = Resisting Cyclic Stress Ratio. [tis a function of the content of fines
(Figure 2, NCEER Workshop, after Seed et al 1985))
Factor of Safety, F, Is:
FL = CRRe/CSRI

Kapafama Container Wharf Project
LIQUEFACTION ANALYSIS (MCE), Mf = 7.5 ; ACC, = 0,359 g
Liquefaction analysis is performed following Seed's Procedure, outlined by Sead et al. (4985),
Seed and Harder (1990), updated NCEER (1997), Martin et al. (1999) and Youd et al. (2001)
EmearVE60 = Energy Ratio to correst to standard 60% Energy
Surcharge = Any surcharge on tap of the ground (psf)
acc. max = maximum peak acceleration at the ground surface (g's)
4Cy = square root of (100 kPa or 1.04427 tsffeffective overburden)
The induced cyclic stress ratlo (CSR i} by a given peak ground acceleration (acc. max} is:
CSR i = 0.65 x acc max. x totas normal stress x rd / {effective normal stress x g)
EMI Project No.
The resisting cyclic stress ratio (CRR 5) is equal to:
CRRr=CRR;5 x Om x K sigma
where: Cm = Earthquake Magnitude Carrection Factor (Blake, NCEER Fable 3, Eq.4)
K sigma = Normaiized resistance factor {Fig. 14 from NCEER Liquefaction workshop, Harder Boufanger)
CRR;,; = Resisting Cyclic Stress Ratio. It is a function of the content of fines
(Figure 2, NCEER Workshop, after Seed et ai 1985))
Factor of Safety, F, is:
F,= CRRr/CSRi

SEISMIC SETTLEMENT CALCULATIONS Kapaiama Container Whart Project EMI Project No. 13-104
UNDER OLE CONDITION, M = 6.5 ; ACC. = 0.0654
FORMULAE:
Corrected blow count {Ny leo = Oy Go" Ce * Ca" Ca * Cor™ Nias Ke = (1-siké))
where: Naw bad Uncorrected fisid blow count Tm =(1*2K 3 ag:
Cu =z Overburden correction fector {Soe Reference 1) {Ka)ome* 20°(N4)% 1/3)
Ce = Energy correction (Referance 2) = (Efficioncy/90%) Gyre 1000°( Kz Jonas On U2}
